items.title: Shocking DNA Study Reveals The Plague Is Prehistoric
items.note:
Yersinia Pestis - the most feared disease in human history - has long been thought to be the Plague of Justinian in 541 AD. But new studies of ancient DNA have revealed traces of Yersinia pestis dating back more than 5000 years.

items.note:
In 1660 the English crown was restored and Charles II began his reign as the 'Merry Monarch'. We might think he would have a taste for decadent foods, but actually, simple small cakes were 'much esteemed' at the Restoration court.

In this video, our Historic Kitchens Team follow 17th Century cook Mrs Stockdel's recipe, figuring out the best method from the simple instructions, to see how 'excellent' they really are. Are these cakes (or biscuits) fit for a king?

items.title: 4 Beluga Whales from Shuttered Canadian Park Arrive at Chicago’s Shedd Aquarium After Rescue (LOOK)
items.note: Four beluga whales that faced an uncertain future when their Canadian park closed have found a safe, professional home at Shedd Aquarium, a reminder that when institutions work together, vulnerable animals don't get left behind. It's a real second chance, hard-won and well-deserved.

items.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
items.note: In a media landscape that can feel relentlessly heavy, a dedicated roundup of genuinely good news is a quiet act of service, proof that positive things are happening every week, even when they don't make the loudest headlines.

items.title: 11-year-old boy shows his American pride by crocheting 8-foot flag using 60 skeins of yarn
items.note: An 11-year-old turning 60 skeins of yarn into an eight-foot crocheted flag is a lovely reminder that creativity, patience, and quiet dedication are alive and well in the next generation.

items.title: From 300 to 3,000: how Australia brought the numbat back
items.note: Australia's numbat population has grown tenfold, from just 300 to 3,000, showing that targeted, committed conservation work can genuinely pull a species back from the edge. This small, striking marsupial is living proof that extinction is not always inevitable.

items.title: Medieval Strip Farming in England
items.note: Those faint ridge-and-furrow undulations still visible in English fields aren't just geography, they're the lasting impression of a feudal fairness system, land carved into strips so no single peasant got all the good soil. The earth itself remembers the medieval argument over who gets which patch.

items.title: Gates of Heaven Synagogue in Madison, Wisconsin
items.note: In 1971, Madisonians literally rolled a 200-ton synagogue through city streets rather than let it fall to a wrecking ball, which may be the most charmingly stubborn act of historic preservation on record. It now hosts weddings on a lakefront, having survived demolition, dentistry, and political campaigning along the way.

items.title: Experimental Alzheimer's Drug That Fights a Tough-to-Target Protein Shows Promise at Slowing Cognitive Decline in a Clinical Trial
items.note: For decades, Alzheimer's research placed its biggest bet on clearing amyloid plaques, but tau tangles, the other villain lurking in the diseased brain, may be just as worthy a target. The quiet implication here is that we may have been fighting only half the war.

items.title: Months After Burglars Stole Crown Jewels From the Louvre, the Apollo Gallery Reopens, Highlighting Its Origins as a Royal Reception Hall
items.note: The Apollo Gallery was designed by Louis XIV as a stage for royal magnificence, so it's almost poetically fitting that its crown jewels were stolen and the hall still managed to upstage the loss. There's something very Sun King about a room that refuses to be outshone.

items.title: Wacky, Mysterious Paintings by This Surrealist Still Influence Contemporary Artists. An Exhibition in London Will Explore the Legacy of René Magritte
items.note: Magritte's pipe wasn't mischief for mischief's sake, it was a quiet philosophical grenade lobbed at the idea that images and reality are the same thing, a question that feels more urgent now than ever. Nearly a century later, we're still staring at screens wondering what, exactly, is a pipe.

items.title: Different moon rocks tell different tales about a long-ago bombardment
items.note: The Apollo mission rocks told us one version of the moon's violent past, but it turns out they may have been narrating a single catastrophic event rather than the whole story. Samples from the far side are gently rewriting the bombardment timeline we thought we knew.

items.title: The golden age of language was a few thousand years ago
items.note: The world once held perhaps ten times as many languages as it does today, meaning the vast majority of human ways of thinking, naming, and dreaming have already gone silent. Language extinction isn't a modern crisis; it's an ancient, ongoing erasure.

items.title: Sometimes Being First Means Seeing the End Before Anyone Else
items.note: Jacob Tsimerman reached the summit of mathematics young enough to see clearly what most prefer not to, that the field may be approaching problems too hard for unaided human minds. A Fields Medal and an existential worry, arriving together.

items.title: Playful Knitted Pillows by Donna Wilson Infuse Interiors with Whimsy and Joy
items.note: Donna Wilson's knitted pillows are the rare decorative object that seems to have an inner life, somewhere between folk art and a friendly hallucination. Small, handmade, and quietly subversive of the idea that interiors should take themselves seriously.

items.title: How to Keep Life from Becoming a Parody of Itself: Simone de Beauvoir on the Art of Growing Older
items.note: De Beauvoir's argument is quietly alarming: the real danger of aging isn't decline, but the slow contraction into a self that no longer reaches outward. This is the essay to read before you catch yourself saying you're too old for something.

items.title: Bean and Lentil Soup with Kale
items.note: This tomato-based bean and lentil soup with kale and mushroom broth is exactly the kind of quietly brilliant, deeply comforting pot you want simmering on the stove, and the fact that it freezes beautifully means future-you will be grateful too.

items.title: The 35 Best Kitchen Deals to Shop This Week from Le Creuset, Nordstrom, and More
items.note: If your kitchen wishlist is longer than your grocery list, this round-up of Le Creuset and Nordstrom deals is a very good reason to treat yourself guilt-free.

items.title: Helen Goh’s recipe for pineapple and mango paletas | The sweet spot
items.note: Paletas, Mexico's gloriously unfussy answer to the ice lolly, are having a well-deserved moment, and this piece makes a compelling case for why your freezer should meet them immediately.

items.title: Squid Ink Spaghetti
items.note: Slow-cooked seafood, a glossy jet-black sauce, and a bright hit of lemon: squid ink spaghetti is the kind of dramatic, deeply satisfying pasta that makes a Tuesday feel like an occasion.

items.title: The War and Treaty: Tiny Desk Concert
items.note: Church-raised and road-hardened, this is music that grabs you by the collar and insists you feel something.

items.title: Nicole Atkins – “Singing In The Mirror”
items.note: The kind of song you rediscover on a playlist and pause whatever you're doing to actually listen.

items.title: Eels – “All Forgotten” & “I’m On Standby”
items.note: Sitting alone in a dimly lit kitchen at 2am, not sad exactly, just quietly acquainted with sadness.

items.title: Melaina Kol – “Idola”
items.note: Like walking through a familiar forest and realizing the trees have quietly rearranged themselves.

items.note: Boot-stomping defiance with a rainbow flag flying out the truck window on a long Texas highway.

items.note: Like finding an old handwritten letter tucked inside a book you forgot you loved.

items.title: Stef Chura Recruits Peter Buck, Mike Mills for New Album
items.note: Late-night indie rock with the restless ache of someone dancing alone in an empty apartment.

items.title: Unreleased John Prine Recordings to Be Compiled In New Collection Found Dogs
items.note: A dusty treasure chest cracked open, letting the warm, bittersweet light of a lost voice spill back into the room.

items.title: Lizzy McAlpine Is Lovestruck on Majestic New Song “The light in the painting”: Stream
items.note: The breathless, overexposed feeling of being newly in love when everything looks slightly too beautiful to be real.

items.title: “Eve of Destruction”: History Never Repeats Itself in These 12 Versions
items.note: The unsettling comfort of an old protest song that sounds like it was written this morning.

items.title: Guitarist Miles Okazaki Expands His Sound with ‘Boomtown’
items.note: The sonic equivalent of a city skyline at dusk, densely layered, quietly ambitious, and entirely its own.

items.title: Eric Cline on The REAL Armageddon
items.note:
Armageddon is more than just a biblical prophecy hailing the end of days. It is a real place: Megiddo, an ancient city that for thousands of years stood at the crossroads of empires, trade routes and wars in the ancient Near East.

In this episode of The Ancients, Tristan Hughes is joined by friend of the show Eric Cline to uncover the true story of the real Armageddon. Why did Megiddo become associated with the world’s final battle? What made this city so strategically important for millennia?

items.title: Maiden Rocket Launch ‘a Grand Success’ for India’s First Private Spaceflight Company
items.note: India's first private spaceflight company reaching orbit is a landmark moment that opens the door to broader, more diverse participation in space exploration beyond a handful of powerful nations. When more of humanity has a stake in reaching the stars, the ambition and ingenuity driving that effort grows with it.

items.title: The upside of the global energy crisis
items.note: Crisis has a way of forcing the change that comfort never would, and the global energy crunch is accelerating the shift toward renewables faster than any policy alone could have managed. The painful disruption is quietly laying the groundwork for a more resilient, independent energy future.

items.title: What It Means to Shape Your Own Space After Prison
items.note: People rebuilding their lives after incarceration deserve spaces that feel like their own, and programs helping them do exactly that restore dignity in one of the most concrete ways possible. It's a reminder that genuine second chances are built not just with words, but with walls, rooms, and a place to call home.

items.title: The Mary Pit Head in Fife, Scotland
items.note: The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80 foot high. Adjacent to the structure is a small outdoor exhibit which showcases equipment from the time, such as coal-cutters, hydraulic roof supports, and carts (both for coal and for miners) that were used in the pit.

items.title: The Payphone at the Edge of the Forest in Altadena, California
items.note: At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone. Not just any payphone, a working payphone. A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles.

items.title: La Casa del Serpentello in Vignale Monferrato, Italy
items.note: What makes the Serpentello House truly special is its unique artistic character. The façade and many of its decorations are entirely covered with handcrafted ceramic tiles, each one unique and personally designed and created by the owner, Luciano Serpentello, in 1925. This remarkable attention to detail transforms the house into a true work of art, giving it a distinctive and unforgettable atmosphere.

items.title: Their Names Were Lost to History. But Now, You Can See What Their Faces May Have Looked Like When They Lived in a Roman-Era City Nearly 2,000 Years Ago
items.note: A new exhibition in Budapest features 16 facial reconstructions based on skulls excavated from the ancient city of Aquincum. Curators don't know the details of their lives, but they've invented backstories to fill in the gaps

items.title: Hunter-Gatherers Brought Trout to a Norwegian Mountain Lake 7,000 Years Ago, the Earliest Known Example of Humans Translocating Fish
items.note: Researchers have long known that the animals were introduced into the region's high-altitude bodies of water, but they weren't sure when it happened or whether humans had a hand. Analyses of recently discovered ancient fish traps provide new clues

items.note: One of the most contested issues in astrophysics is our understanding of the Universe’s expansion. Adam Riess discusses why there is disagreement and what might resolve it. The post How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding?

items.note: Every four years, the math world’s preeminent awards are given to the finest minds of a generation. The young winners, all under 40 on the first day of the year, have reshaped mathematics and theoretical computer science. Here are their stories.

items.title: Patrick Dougherty’s ‘Stickwork’ Installations Conjure Connections with Nature
items.note: For more than four decades, the artist has reimagined the technical and sculptural capabilities of one simple material: twigs. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

items.title: Carl Jung on Creativity
items.note: Jung's uncomfortable answer to what creativity actually costs us is that it demands the whole self, including the parts we've forgotten we carry. This piece sits quietly with the paradox that our deepest creative material lives precisely where we can't deliberately reach it.

items.title: Cape Verde's women musicians keep Cesária Évora's legacy alive

items.title: Earliest Humans: We Were Not Alone
items.note:
For most of human history, we were not alone. Human evolution was shaped by multiple human species living side by side, from Neanderthals in Europe to Denisovans in Asia, before all but one disappeared.

In this episode of The Ancients, Tristan Hughes is joined by Ella Al-Shamahi to explore the story of the early humans who once shared our world. How did these different species evolve? Did they compete or coexist? And what do the latest discoveries reveal about the tangled story of human evoluti

items.title: How Tudor Sailors Survived a 7 Month Journey | Salt Pork Experiment
items.note:
In the 16th century, everybody salted their food to make it last the winter. It was salt that allowed for the golden age of exploration, taking European explorers on journeys of 6 months or even four years. In this video, our Tudor historian experiments with a Tudor style salt pork, salting the meat and storing it in a barrel in a cupboard for seven months. Will the salt pork last the winter and be good enough to eat in spring? Let's find out. 

Please note that this is an historical experiment

items.title: Paralyzed Man Can Feed Himself and Pet His Dog Again Thanks to Double Neural Bypass Surgery
items.note: A double neural bypass surgery has restored a paralyzed man's ability to feed himself and feel his dog's fur again, a reminder that medical science is quietly achieving things that once seemed impossible. These small, intimate moments of reconnection are exactly what innovation should be fighting for.

items.title: Japanese botanist found the secret to reforestation in ancient shrines. The results were undeniable.
items.note: A Japanese botanist discovered that ancient shrine forests, left largely untouched for centuries, hold the blueprint for successful reforestation, meaning nature itself has been quietly preserving the answer all along. It's a beautiful example of traditional wisdom and modern science finding common ground beneath the canopy.

items.title: Fashion’s dirty secret: the EU just banned brands from incinerating unsold clothes
items.note: The EU's ban on incinerating unsold clothing closes a long-ignored loophole that saw perfectly good garments destroyed by the millions each year. It's a meaningful, enforceable step toward a fashion industry that finally has to take responsibility for what it makes.

items.title: Fuel Tank Orca Mural in Palmer Station, Antarctica
items.note: The fuel tank mural at Palmer Station is one of the most recognizable landmarks at this remote Antarctic research base. Painted on the station’s large fuel tank, the mural features a leaping orca and adds a unique piece of public art to the otherwise industrial scenery. Visible from the water as ships approach the station, it has become a familiar symbol of Palmer Station for residents and visitors alike.

items.title: Suns Cinema in Washington, D.C.
items.note: Set on one of the best streets in Washington, DC, this neighborhood gem has the perfect mix of an impressive film selection and delicious cocktails. Affordable tickets to see a big variety of movies screened almost daily make this spot worth visiting. Independently run, the owner David Cabrera shows films he likes that range in topic, format, and style.

items.title: Explore More: Field Notes on Exploration, Discovery, and Adventure

items.note: Isaac "Ike" Towell was born on November 25, 1849 and died, by suicide, on February 22, 1934 at the age of 83 in Columbus, Texas. Ike was the city marshal of Columbus in 1885 and a lifelong "infidel". He was known as a man of unwavering principals and had many friends near and far.

Officially filming in 1981, Steven Spielberg shot many scenes of this cherished movie in suburban areas of Los Angeles county, such as the San Fernando Valley.

items.title: Drops of Rain in Étretat, France
items.note: Situated within the Jardin Emotions of the Jardins d'Étretat, one can find the "Drops of Rain" sculptures by Samuel Salcedo, that pout, grin, and stick their tongues out at visitors. These sculptures are nestled into bushes in a way that is supposed to resemble oyster habitats, as an homage to Marie Antoinette's oyster farm by this garden. They display the range of human emotion with a sort of lightheartedness and absurdity that makes Étretat feel even more magical.

items.note: Even off the clock, some people run toward danger without hesitation, a reminder that quiet heroism lives in ordinary communities. Three children are home safe because one person chose to act.

items.note: A painless, rapid brush test for oral cancer could make early detection far more accessible, giving patients a crucial head start when treatment is most effective.

items.title: Osaka's midpoint in Osaka, Japan
items.note: When driving around a country you will find signs telling you the distance to surrounding places. People use these signs without much thought, but of course such a measurement needs to be based on something. In the case of Osaka, it is a shiny golden pin.

items.title: The Canal Vulva (Kutkunst) in Rotterdam, Netherlands
items.note: Street art is ubiquitous in Rotterdam, coming in all shapes and sizes, covering a vast array of topics in its own unique way. The canal vulva is one such piece that has fascinated passersby for over a decade. Not much is known about this piece, apart from the fact that it is a female reproductive organ that was fastened to the wall close to some space invader mosaics.

items.title: Bandai Co. Head Office in Taito City, Japan
items.note: The key to success for toy companies is their product but also their IP. Bandai Co. excels in both, as they have some of the best-known characters in their roster.

items.title: Wabash & Erie Canal Park in Delphi, Indiana
items.note: If you own a mule and it happens to be named Cal, Gal, Hal, Pal, Val, or preferably Sal, grab your towrope and head over to Delphi, Indiana, to check out the Wabash and Erie Canal Park and Museum. Opened in 2004, the museum features twelve interactive galleries tracing the history of the Wabash and Erie Canal from its inception and construction to its use and eventual closure. The park grounds have an extensive trail system and an 1850s pioneer village, where craftsmen ply their trades during canal season (mid-May through mid-September).

items.title: A Volunteer Stumbled Upon a Strange Document in the U.K. National Archives. It Turned Out to Be a Rare Copy of the Declaration of Independence Printed in July 1776

items.note: The strange-looking creatures are one of two known mammals that use a social structure like that of bees and ants. A ruling rodent maintains power over her colony via one key chemical compound, according to new research

items.title: A Massive Flow of Rock and Mud Tumbled Down Mount Rainier Centuries Ago. Scientists Pinpointed Its Date to Better Understand the Volcano's Complex Threats
items.note: In a recent study, researchers dated a historic lahar called the Electron Mudflow to the summer of 1507, establishing a way to determine when past geologic events around the Puget Sound occurred

items.title: Ape cuddles could prevent conflicts
items.note: Humans could learn from chimpanzees and bonobos, who turn to group members for physical comfort during tense moments.

The Palacio de Vistalegre Arena, originally conceived as a bullring, was inaugurated in 2000 to replace an earlier structure from the early 20th century that was demolished in 1995. With a capacity of 14,000 people, it has hosted concerts, political rallies, and other large-scale events.

The „Liebesweg“ in Poppenhausen was the first love-themed path in Germany.

items.title: After 5-yo Diagnosed with Rare Disease, Little Sister Wanted to Be Her ‘Brave’ Bone Marrow Donor
items.note: A young child's instinct, when her sister needed saving, was simply to help, reminding us that courage and compassion can live in the smallest of hearts. Stories like this show how love within families can rise to meet even the most frightening medical challenges.

items.title: ‘A place of hope and solutions’: the building promising a better future
items.note: Somewhere, people are actively designing spaces dedicated to real-world solutions and collective optimism, proof that hope isn't passive, but something we can literally build. That kind of intentional, grounded thinking is exactly what difficult times call for.

items.title: A flight attendant with 11 years in the air says she’ll never wear shorts on a plane
items.note: A seasoned flight attendant sharing hard-won, practical knowledge is a quiet reminder that everyday expertise, freely given, genuinely keeps people safer. It's the kind of no-fuss, neighbour-helping-neighbour wisdom that never goes out of style.

items.title: In Cuba’s fuel crisis, solar tricycles took the streets
items.note: When fuel ran short in Cuba, communities didn't simply wait, they pedalled forward on solar-powered tricycles, turning scarcity into a cleaner, more human-scaled way of getting around. It's a vivid example of how necessity can nudge us toward solutions that are better for people and the planet alike.

items.title: Museo Casa del Mendrugo in Puebla, Mexico
items.note: A Jesuit charity house named after breadcrumbs is charming enough, but the real twist is that when workers dug up the courtyard in 2010, they found a burial site two millennia older than the building itself. The mansion was just the newest tenant.

items.title: The House of David Bowie in Berlin, Germany
items.note: Bowie wrote 'Heroes' and 'Low' behind that unremarkable oak door in a Schöneberg tenement, which still has its original double-keyholes designed to compel residents to lock up, a small, stubborn piece of early 20th-century distrust preserved in cast iron.

items.title: Teddy Bear Park in Stillwater, Minnesota
items.note: Stillwater, Minnesota made the quietly radical decision to fill a public park with life-sized teddy bears, a reminder that the thing we gave children to cuddle is, in the wild, an apex predator.

items.title: Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
items.note: Long before the Northern Pacific laid its tracks through Yakima Canyon, Native peoples had already mapped the same logic into the land, the river gap was simply too obvious a path for any era to ignore.

items.title: The Tower of London Plans to Turn Its Moat Into a ‘Green Classroom’ and Show Off Its Architecture Archives in a Major Renovation
items.note: The Tower of London is turning its ancient moat, once a stinking, strategic barrier, into a green classroom, which feels like exactly the right fate for a ditch that outlived its purpose by about 600 years.

items.title: An Unusual-Looking Raccoon Nicknamed Jimothy Is Winning Hearts Across the Internet for His Short, Round Body
items.note: Jimothy's unusually compact, round silhouette likely comes from a rare congenital spine condition, and yet Seattle's Ballard neighborhood has adopted him as a local celebrity, proof that the internet's appetite for a distinguished-looking raccoon is essentially bottomless.

items.title: A Man With Paralysis Has Regained Feeling and Movement in His Hands After an Experimental Brain Implant
items.note: After a brain implant and months of patient retraining, a man with paralysis can now feel the sensation of touch again, not just move, but *feel*, which is the detail that quietly changes everything about what this experiment means.

items.note: The new women's pro baseball league didn't reach for generic team names, each one was shaped around a real, specific woman: an honorary firefighter, a civil rights activist, a first basewoman, a physician, each city's history suddenly legible in a jersey.

items.title: Pompeii’s survivors shine in a new documentary series
items.note: We've long told Pompeii's story through its dead, but this documentary series, and Tom Hiddleston, somehow, turns its attention to the survivors, the people who fled the ash and then had to figure out what came next.

items.title: Presence, Memory, and Time Merge in the Surreal Works of ‘Architects of Absence’
items.note: An exhibition built around absence sounds like it risks disappearing into its own concept, but the most interesting tension here is that 'Architects of Absence' insists on presence, of memory, of time, of the people who left the gaps.

items.title: Notes on Complexity: A Buddhist Scientist on the Murmuration of Being
items.note: This essay asks what it actually means to say 'you are made of stardust', and then keeps pulling the thread until the self dissolves into quantum foam and something stranger takes its place. Settle in for the rare piece of science writing that leaves you feeling both smaller and somehow more whole.

items.title: Iced Sesame Noodles
items.note: These iced sesame noodles are your answer to sweltering evenings, a creamy, cold, endlessly customisable bowl that comes together with very little fuss and disappears even faster.

items.title: Here’s Your Signature Cake Flavor, Based on Your Birthday
items.note: A delightfully silly bit of edible astrology that's worth a click if only to argue with the results.

items.title: Rachel Roddy’s recipe for pasta with courgette, onion and raw tomato salsa | A kitchen in Rome
items.note: Rachel Roddy reminds us that the best summer cooking barely qualifies as cooking, and that's precisely the point.

items.title: Hot Wieners
items.note: A deep dive into Rhode Island's fiercely specific hot wiener tradition, proof that regional food pride is always worth understanding.

items.title: Albums from Mike Campbell and Low Cut Connie draw inspiration from rock's past
items.note: Campbell co-wrote a number of hits for Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers. His new band is The Dirty Knobs, and their album is Mission of Mercy. Low Cut Connie's new album is Livin in the USA.

items.title: The Forgotten Air Campaign: Korea, 1950
items.note:
The air war in Korea is often remembered for the first jet on jet combats and the dreaded “MiG alley.”  But a far less remembered part of the air war, before a MiG entered the fray, represented a key, if almost completely overlooked, moment in one of the hottest times of the Cold War.

items.title: Henry VIII's Tennis Court at Hampton Court Palace
items.note:
Did you know that Henry VIII's palace had a tennis court? Tennis, or to be precise, lawn tennis, is derived from an ancient game. Once enjoyed by the masses, it later became the preserve of the wealthy. We call this sport Real Tennis to distinguish it from the modern game. And it was this that was incredibly popular at the Tudor court.

items.note:

items.title: Scientists Finally Figured Out What Love Is For
items.note:
The dumb things we do for love make a lot more sense than you think…
↓↓↓ More info and sources below ↓↓↓

Love makes people do ridiculous things: quit jobs, cross continents, build monuments, and ignore every red flag in sight. But what if that's exactly what love evolved to do? This video explores the neuroscience, psychology, and evolutionary biology of romance to explain why love can feel irrational, and why that may have helped make us human.

In this video I rank some of the most common gemstones from best to worst based on durability, aesthetics, and value for money.

items.title: Biotech Startup Breeds Blight Tolerant American Chestnut Without Any Genetic Defects
items.note: The American chestnut, once a cornerstone of Eastern forests, was nearly wiped out by blight a century ago, and now scientists have bred a healthy, blight-tolerant tree that could restore this beloved species to the wild. It's a quiet triumph for both conservation and the patient, determined people who never stopped believing the forest could heal.

items.title: The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave
items.note: A simple, low-tech underground cooling system is helping communities keep food fresh without electricity, proving that ancient wisdom and clever design can offer real solutions to modern climate challenges. It's a hopeful reminder that not every answer to a big problem has to be complicated or expensive.

items.title: What If We Never Built Another New Building?
items.note: A growing movement of architects and planners is making the case for retrofitting and reimagining existing buildings rather than constantly building new ones, slashing the carbon footprint of construction in the process. It turns out that caring for what we already have, in housing, as in nature, might be one of the most radical and hopeful acts we can take.

items.title: Saihō-ji in Kyoto, Japan
items.note: The city of Kyoto has over 1,600 Buddhist temples. While this is a very large number, it often feels like this isn’t enough. Famous sites such as Kinkaku-ji , Kiyomizu-dera , and Ryōan-ji are often seen over the shoulders of hundreds of other visitors, who tend to be cropped out of social media posts.

items.title: The Limberlost Cabin in Geneva, Indiana
items.note: The Limberlost Cabin was the home of famed Indiana author Gene Stratton-Porter, who designed and lived in the home in Geneva, Indiana, from 1895 to 1913. Mrs. Stratton-Porter was born in nearby Wabash County, Indiana, in 1863.

items.title: Corrimony Chambered Cairn in Corrimony, Scotland
items.note: Corrimony Chambered Cairn, nestled in the Glen Urquhart area of the Scottish Highlands, is a striking example of a Bronze Age burial site, thought to date back more than 4,000 years. This cairn is part of the Clava type, a style commonly associated with the region, and it offers a glimpse into ancient funerary practices and beliefs. The cairn features a circular stone structure about 15 meters in diameter, with a passage leading to a central burial chamber.

items.title: Elmhurst Sculpture Garden in Queens, New York
items.note: The strips of green next to the highway offramps tend to be pretty desolate, populated with weeds and trash. The pocket next to where the Long Island Expressway lets drivers off onto Queens Boulevard was no exception, until a group of sculptors decided to carve out a space for local art. The space was already being used sporadically by people in the neighborhood, but it was local artist Susan Vario who asked another artist, Yvonne Shortt, to collaborate on a project to turn it into a true community space.

items.title: Did These Ancient Egyptian Princesses Wield Weapons and Train in Martial Arts, Just Like the Men of Their Time?
items.note: A new analysis of their bones suggests they were buried alongside arrows, maces and daggers because they had repeatedly used those weapons in life

items.title: Koalas Are at Risk of Extinction in Some Parts of Australia. Can Freezing Their Sperm and Eggs Help Save the Iconic Marsupials?
items.note: Scientists are preserving the animals' reproductive cells in liquid nitrogen in a bid to boost genetic diversity in future populations

items.title: Poop From Southern Right Whales Reveals That the Animals Might Be More Resilient to Climate Change Than We Thought
items.note: Scientists long believed that these whales, which swim throughout the Southern Hemisphere, mainly feasted on krill and small crustaceans called copepods, which are disappearing from the warming ocean. A new study suggests that the cetaceans have a more varied, flexible diet

items.title: In painted caves, ancient human DNA clings to the walls
items.note: Scientists recovered ancient human DNA from cave walls for the first time. Refined, the method could help link prehistoric paintings to their makers.

items.title: AI ‘ghosts’ can comfort mourners — even when the bots get the facts wrong
items.note: A small study suggests emotional fit, not perfect accuracy, may make chatbot versions of dead loved ones feel real.

items.note: The near-extinction of the bison sparked an industry that left almost nothing of the animals behind. The post What Happened to the Bones of the American Bison? appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.note: Extremophiles that thrive in the most unforgiving environments aren’t just biological curiosities. Understanding their resilience has many implications for us. The post The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.note: John D. Boswell, a musician and filmmaker who works as MELODYSHEEP, created the film from videos and images published by NASA. Do stories and artists like this matter to you?

items.note: This illustrated meditation asks why life exists at all, not as a scientific problem to solve, but as a strangeness to sit inside. It moves from mitochondria to consciousness with the unhurried wonder of someone who genuinely can't believe any of this is happening.

items.title: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World - The Truth Behind the Legends
items.note:
Six of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World are gone. But almost everything you think you know about them is wrong.

The Hanging Gardens of Babylon may never have existed. The famous image of the Colossus of Rhodes straddling the harbour? Invented by medieval artists 1,400 years after it fell. The pyramids weren't built by slaves. And several of these "lost" wonders aren't really lost, pieces of them are hiding inside buildings you can visit today.

Skin, hair and cleanliness was a measure of your health, and your status, and for women, their fertility too. In this video, Historic Kitchens Experts Natalie and Anya delve into the world of 14th century hair and beauty, and try out a few recipes from the time. Despite ingredients such as egg yolk, lard and white wine, Anya and Natalie are both impressed with the results.

items.title: Pigeon Walks Up to Firefighters Who Offer Oxygen to ‘Clear its Lungs’
items.note: A pigeon instinctively seeking help from firefighters, and the crew pausing to treat it, is a small but genuine reminder that compassion for all living things is alive and well. These moments of unlikely connection between humans and wildlife are worth cherishing.

items.title: 27 English words people have a hard time enunciating properly, even native speakers
items.note: Language is endlessly humbling, and there's something quietly unifying about the fact that even lifelong English speakers stumble over the same words. It's a gentle nudge that nobody has it all figured out, and that's perfectly fine.

items.title: What watching soccer does to your body, according to science
items.note: Science is finding that shared experiences like watching sport can have measurable positive effects on our bodies and sense of belonging, a good reminder that community and joy are not just nice to have, but genuinely good for us.

items.title: The Darß Front Doors Path in Prerow, Germany
items.note: These aren't merely decorative, the vivid, intricately carved doors of the Darß peninsula were a fisherman's way of broadcasting identity and pride in a world where most things were salt-bleached and plain. UNESCO agreeing, three centuries later, feels like a long-overdue knock on the right door.

items.title: Tongyili & Tongdeli in Su Zhou Shi, China
items.note: The Green Gang ran Shanghai's underworld with terrifying efficiency, yet its most powerful bosses quietly kept their roots in a genteel canal town known for silk and scholars, the kind of origin story that makes you look twice at any sleepy backwater. Suzhou's still waters, it turns out, ran very deep.

items.title: Dongfangzhimen - The Big Pants Building in Suzhou Shi, China
items.note: A skyscraper every five days is staggering enough, but the real detail here is that China's breakneck skyline ambitions have produced buildings so architecturally audacious, or baffling, that locals nicknamed one 'the Big Pants.' Modernism at that velocity apparently has its own dress code.

items.title: The Dog Line in Eaglehawk Neck, Australia
items.note: A hundred meters of chained, snarling dogs was all that stood between hundreds of desperate convicts and freedom, a psychological barrier as much as a physical one, since the mere sound of barking was the real lock. It's a grimly ingenious solution that says everything about how Port Arthur's keepers understood fear.

items.title: How to Be a Lichen: Adaptive Strategies for the Vulnerabilities of Being Human from Nature’s Tiny Titans of Tenacity
items.note: This essay uses the quiet, stubborn life of lichen, part fungus, part algae, wholly neither, to ask what it might mean to thrive not despite vulnerability but through an entirely different arrangement of self. Maria Popova turns a childhood mustache of Bulgarian moss into a meditation on symbiosis, resilience, and the strange dignity of being an in-between thing.

items.title: Cherry Tomato Tart with Burnt Scallion Ricotta
items.note: With an all-butter crust, a swipe of chili crisp, ricotta folded with deeply roasted scallions and fresh chives, and blistered cherry tomatoes on top, this tart is peak summer with very little standing between you and something genuinely beautiful.

items.title: Melon Tomato Salad
items.note: A Tajín vinaigrette on melon and tomato is the kind of summer pairing that makes you wonder why you ever ate either fruit any other way.

items.title: Rachel Roddy’s homage to Michèle Roberts’ recipe for chicken saute with tomatoes and mushrooms
items.note: A Napoleonic classic stripped of its fuss, Rachel Roddy's quiet homage proves that the best cooking is often just getting out of the recipe's way.

items.title: What’s the Point of a Food Pyramid Anyway?
items.note: A sharp, timely look at why a government-issued triangle was never quite equipped to hold the full, glorious complexity of how humans actually eat.

items.note: Like sitting cross-legged between two worlds, the chaos of a city that never sleeps and the stillness of a place where time moves like incense smoke.

That's right. It's not often that I am utterly entranced and motivated to drop what I'm doing and create a new cosplay, but watching Guillermo Del Toro's Frankenstein got my creature-loving brain FIRED UP.
So please enjoy this lengthy quest of me trying many new methods, wig styling, prosthetic makeup, and more!
I am taking December off, so I will see you in the new year!

items.title: Pharaoh’s Daughters Were Skilled at Archery 4,000 Years ago, Reveals New Research
items.note: New research revealing that ancient Egyptian princesses were accomplished archers 4,000 years ago reminds us that human skill, strength, and ambition have never been the sole preserve of one gender. History keeps offering up these quiet corrections, and every one of them expands our sense of what people are capable of.

items.title: World's Largest Tortilla in Tlalnepantla de Baz, Mexico
items.note: A 120-kilo tortilla was never meant to be eaten, it was a canvas, sculpted into an edible (well, technically) monument to Mexican identity by a single art teacher working in corn dough. There's something quietly radical about turning the most ordinary staple into the grandest possible gesture.

items.title: Roadside America Museum in Hillsboro, Texas
items.note: Carroll Estes was collecting 1950s Americana before nostalgia had even decided the '50s were worth missing, which means he wasn't chasing a trend, he was just paying attention when everyone else was throwing things away. That's the rarest kind of curator.

items.title: Major Adams Monument in Johannesburg, California
items.note: The X-15 pilots were technically astronauts, touching the edge of space, yet the program has nearly dissolved from public memory, overshadowed by the moonshot it quietly helped make possible. A monument on a California roadside may be all that marks the spot where that story almost disappeared entirely.

items.title: Kilmodan Stones in Glendaruel, Scotland
items.note: The Picts left no written language, so these carved sandstone slabs in a sleepy Scottish glen are effectively the closest thing to a sentence they ever finished. Whatever they were trying to say has been weathering slowly for over a thousand years, still legible enough to unsettle you.

items.title: Borges on How to Conquer Time
items.note: Borges offers something stranger and more consoling than the usual advice about mortality: not how to manage time, but how to recognize that you *are* it. Set aside the half-hour and let him dissolve the distance between the river and the person drowning in it.

items.title: Clairo Shares “Losing Pride” Demo To Benefit Climate Justice
items.note: A quietly urgent bedroom whisper sent out into a burning world, fragile but purposeful.

items.title: Ma Rainey, Fats Waller and the Jazzy, Bluesy, Showy Roar of the Roaring ’20s
items.note: Smoke-stained speakeasy energy that makes you want to order something strong and let the century wash over you.

items.title: What Exactly Is Worcestershire Sauce?
items.note:
Worcestershire sauce: It's salty, tangy, funky, fishy and almost impossible to spell or pronounce. But while you’ve almost certainly tasted it... do you actually know what’s in it?
One of the oldest bottled sauces in America, this mysterious condiment turns up everywhere from burgers to Bloody Marys and it has a history that’s far stranger than you’d expect.

So on this episode of Weird History Food, we're going to try and answer the questions: What is Worcestershire sauce and how did it become

items.note:
Deadliest Battles for Survival on the African Savannah | FULL EPISODE | Wildlife Documentary is a breathtaking nature animal documentary that begins on the sun scorched plains of the Serengeti and climbs all the way to the frozen roof of the world, asking one brutal question that every predator on Earth must answer: how do you survive when everything around you is built to kill you.

Through cinematic footage and educational storytelling, this documentary reveals that nature never gives the same

items.title: Others Dreamed of His Basketball Career But He Wanted to Help People–He’s Now a Texas Police Officer
items.note: In a world that often pushes people toward fame and fortune, this young man chose quiet, meaningful service to his community instead. It's a reminder that real fulfilment comes from following your own calling, not someone else's dream.

items.title: After 33 years, two old-school pen pals an ocean apart finally got to meet in person
items.note: Thirty-three years of handwritten letters across an ocean is a testament to the enduring power of genuine human connection, and the moment those two friends finally embraced in person proves that real friendship doesn't need Wi-Fi to survive.

items.title: Penshurst Medieval Dole Table in Penshurst, England
items.note: A stone table in a churchyard where the poor would receive food or clothing on the darkest day of the year, most have vanished, making Penshurst's survivor a quiet monument to organized compassion. There's something almost haunting about a table set for centuries of forgotten guests.

items.title: Knute Rockne Statue in Notre Dame, Indiana
items.note: The phrase 'Win one for the Gipper' has been borrowed by politicians and coaches alike, yet most people couldn't tell you who the Gipper actually was, a young Notre Dame halfback named George Gipp who died at 25. Rockne reportedly held the deathbed request in reserve for eight years, waiting for exactly the right losing locker room to unleash it.

items.title: Anna Freitag Memorial in Scheiben, Austria
items.note: A shy young postmistress, lonely enough to accept Sunday dinner invitations from strangers, was memorialized by the very village that took her in, which says something tender about how small communities quietly notice the isolated among them.

items.title: The Jelling Stone Replica in London, England
items.note: The original Jelling Stone is so weathered its colors have nearly vanished, but the 1948 London replica was painted in the vivid reds, blues, and blacks scholars believe it once wore, meaning a cast tucked beside Regent's Park may be the closest thing we have to seeing the Viking Age monument as it actually looked.

items.title: How to Love: Legendary Zen Buddhist Teacher Thich Nhat Hanh on Mastering the Art of “Interbeing”
items.note: Thich Nhat Hanh dismantles the comfortable myth that love is something we simply feel, arguing instead that it is a skill, one most of us have never been taught. A quiet, unsettling, and ultimately tender reckoning with how much of our loving is actually hurting.

items.title: 'Les Miserable' area concert wraps up world tour in New York City
items.note: After 17 countries and audiences of thousands, the concert celebrating the 40th anniversary of the landmark musical is drawing to a close.

items.title: 10 photographers share favorite moments from the Tiny Desk Contest Tour
items.note: Ten photographers share their favorite memories from the 2026 Tiny Desk Contest On The Road tour, which was headlined by this year's winner, the Dallas hip-hop group Cure for Paranoia.

items.note: Artists create, even during times of chaos. In response to Trump policies and ICE's incursions, arts organizations are leaning in to new ways of fostering community in ways large and small.

items.note: A weekly reminder that people are still carving out space for reflection, wonder, and a little cosmic curiosity, proof that the human need for meaning and self-examination is alive and well.

items.title: Saint-Thibault Chapel in La Chapelle-au-Mans, France
items.note: This chapel didn't gradually fall into ruin, it *began* as one, materializing on 17th-century maps already ancient and broken, with no record of who built it or why. It's as if history simply forgot to include the beginning.

items.title: Steve Ovett's Foot in Brighton, England
items.note: Of an entire bronze athlete, only the foot survives, which is either a poetic tribute to what made him great, or the universe's most pointed piece of public art criticism.

items.title: Humphrey the M5 Camel in East Bower, England
items.note: Humphrey receives fan mail addressed to nothing more than 'The Camel, M5 Junction 23 and 24', proof that a fiberglass dromedary in a Somerset field has achieved a level of celebrity most humans never will.

items.title: Statue of Lennie Gwyther and His Horse Ginger Mick in Leongatha, Australia
items.note: When a nine-year-old single-handedly kept a farm running in his injured father's absence, the only reward he wanted was to ride his horse 600 miles to watch a bridge open, which he then did, becoming a national sensation along the way.

items.title: Who owns your bones?
items.note: The question of who legally and morally owns human remains after death has shadowed medicine for centuries, with countless individuals, disproportionately from marginalized communities, studied without consent or dignity. The reckoning, it turns out, is still very much underway.

items.title: The Art of the Sacred Pause and Despair as a Catalyst of Regeneration
items.note: This essay makes a quiet, radical argument: that the moments when life seems to collapse into paralysis are not failures of resilience but the necessary dark between one version of yourself and the next. It asks what it might look like to stop fighting the pause, and trust it.

items.title: Mythology Expert Breaks Down Every Ancient Greek Constellation
items.note: Professor Peter Meineck treats the 48 Greek constellations not as trivia but as a working system, one the ancients used for navigation, farming, and timekeeping. The organizing framework of eight groupings gives a genuinely new way to see the night sky rather than a random mythology dump. It's an hour well spent if you've ever looked up and felt like you were missing context.

items.title: How Every Major Pizza Chain Came To Be | Compilation
items.note: Nearly two hours of the actual origin stories behind Domino's, Pizza Hut, Little Caesars, and others, less food tourism, more business history told through immigrant ambition and postwar American appetite. Good background listening if you enjoy the 'how did this become everywhere' genre of corporate storytelling.

items.title: India vs China, Explained
items.note: Johnny Harris maps the India-China rivalry through its least-understood dimension: the 2,100-mile disputed border and the military infrastructure both sides have been quietly building along it. He grounds the geopolitics in specific geography and recent flashpoints, making a genuinely complex relationship legible without oversimplifying it.

items.title: Edinburgh - 1700s
items.note: Matt Reconstructs History rebuilds 1700s Edinburgh, before the planned New Town was built, using AI visuals layered against period maps, paintings, and engravings, giving you a real sense of how dense and vertical the old city actually was. The Nor Loch, the crowded High Street, the logic of building upward on a volcanic ridge, it all clicks into place in under 25 minutes.

items.title: How to define the conversation | Charles Duhigg
items.note: Charles Duhigg distills a single, underused communication skill: deciding what kind of conversation you're actually in before it goes sideways. At under three minutes, it's the kind of reframe that makes you immediately rethink your last difficult exchange. Practical and precise.

items.title: Ranking Classic Philosophy Books (with Joe Folley)
items.note: Jared Henderson and Joe Folley work through canonical philosophy texts, Plato, Aristotle, Marcus Aurelius, Augustine, arguing seriously about which ones still hold up and which are more monument than reading experience. The tier-list format keeps a four-hour runtime moving, and the disagreements are substantive enough to make you want to revisit books you thought you knew.

items.title: A FULL BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, and Social Commentary in Pluribus
items.note: Vince Gilligan's Apple TV+ series 'Pluribus' rewards close reading, and Lucas Blue does the work of mapping its symbols and social commentary systematically rather than impressionistically. If you've finished the show and felt there was more under the surface, this is the breakdown that will confirm or sharpen your instincts.

items.title: I Started a Garden With ZERO Budget - Here's What Happened
items.note: GrowVeg's Ben tests whether you can start a productive garden with literally no money, leaning on cuttings, seed-saving, and supermarket herb rescues. The constraints make it more instructive than a standard gardening tutorial, you see which shortcuts actually work. Fourteen minutes with real, replicable results.

items.title: Beyond the Chateau - Our Family's Cozy Christmas in the Italian Alps
items.note: The family behind the chateau renovation channel trades their French project for a low-key Christmas in the Italian Alps, and the result is quieter and more personal than their usual content. If you follow them for the lifestyle as much as the construction, this is a warm detour worth the 16 minutes.

items.title: Homegrown Catnip Lotion Proves to Be An Effective Mosquito Repellent in Rural Uganda
items.note: A plant anyone can grow in their garden is proving to repel mosquitoes as effectively as synthetic chemicals, offering rural communities in Uganda an affordable, homegrown shield against disease. It's a beautiful reminder that nature often holds practical solutions we've barely begun to explore.

items.title: In just 15 days, this Ohio woman crocheted her own wedding dress and the wedding party’s attire
items.note: One woman's decision to crochet her entire wedding wardrobe in 15 days is a joyful testament to what human hands and determination can create outside the pressures of consumer culture. There's something quietly radical about choosing craft over convenience for one of life's biggest moments.

items.title: Podcast Transcript July 17th, 2026— Wildfire satellites, Seine city cooling, and parenting securely attached kids
items.note: From satellites tracking wildfires in real time to a major city harnessing its river to cool urban streets, these emerging tools show that communities are finding genuinely creative ways to adapt and protect the places we love.

items.title: The Solgrylle at the Blacksmith's Bridge in Aarhus, Denmark
items.note: Smedens Bro , "The Blacksmith's Bridge", is a pedestrian and cycling bridge in central Aarhus. It was built in 1911 at the initiative of the local ironsmith and businessman Hans Peter Jensen, who funded its construction to improve access for residents of his neighbourhood. Spanning the Aarhus River, the light grey railings rise and curve outward at both ends, framing the city's coat of arms.

items.title: Borden Avenue Bridge in Queens, New York
items.note: New York City is a city of the water, built on a series of islands criss-crossed by rivers and streams. So the city is also populated by almost 800 bridges, from giant spans like the Verrazzano to smaller wooden trestles that might only cross a tiny creek. In the case of the Borden Avenue Bridge, this doesn’t mean it’s boring or unimpressive.

items.title: Grace Episcopal Church of City Island in Bronx, New York
items.note: Many New Yorkers will be familiar with Manhattan’s Grace Church, a French Gothic masterpiece built in 1846-1847 on Broadway. Or, if they’re from Brooklyn, perhaps they’re more acquainted with the Grace Church on Hicks Street, which was built in 1847 when the other Grace Church’s then-new location proved too far a trip for some faithful. However, for parishioners in the Bronx, specifically in the small seafaring community of City Point, Grace Episcopal Church on City Island Avenue is the one true bearer of the popular name, and this National Historic Place just so happens to have been built around the same time as its two more famous namesakes, in 1849.

Sometimes it takes the smallest person in the room to remind neighbours they belong to one another.

items.title: In pictures: How forensic science is helping tackle wildlife poaching
items.note: Forensic science is now being turned on poachers the same way it's used to solve human crimes, giving wildlife defenders a powerful new toolkit to catch traffickers and protect endangered species. It's a quiet but meaningful sign that the legal world is taking the destruction of wildlife as seriously as it deserves.

items.title: Doctors blamed her seizures on anxiety. An AI chatbot finally helped her figure out the real cause.
items.note: When years of dismissed symptoms finally found an explanation through an unexpected tool, it opened a conversation about how emerging technology can fill the gaps left by an overstretched medical system. For the many people whose conditions go unrecognised, stories like this are a genuine reason to keep pushing for answers.

items.title: Tampons Are Becoming a Valuable Medical Tool
items.note: Researchers are finding that a familiar, low-cost everyday item could have real diagnostic and therapeutic uses in medicine, a reminder that innovation doesn't always require expensive new inventions. Practical, accessible solutions like this have the potential to reach patients who need help most.

items.title: ScottCakes in Provincetown, Massachusetts
items.note: In a pink-walled basement on Commercial Street, ScottCakes sells one item and nothing else: a golden butter cake under a swirl of pink vanilla buttercream. There are no rotating flavors, no seasonal specials, no coffee menu to round out the order. One cupcake, made one way, on purpose.

items.title: Ancient Historian Answers Your Greek Myth Questions
items.note: Classicist Daisy Dunn tackles the questions people actually Google about Greek myths, not just what they are, but whether ancient Greeks genuinely believed them and how they functioned socially and religiously. It's structured around real audience questions, so it moves efficiently through the material rather than padding a lecture. Good entry point if you want substance without a full university course.

items.title: 10 Amazing Buildings That Once Were!
items.note: This video uses AI reconstruction to show what ten significant lost buildings, including the original Globe Theatre and the Bastille, actually looked like before they disappeared, pairing visuals with the history of why each one vanished. The reconstructions make it easier to understand the architectural and cultural scale of what's been lost. A genuinely useful visual supplement to history you may already know in outline.

items.title: Clearing out the Chateau Attic, the start of MAJOR renovation.
items.note: Clearing a chateau attic sounds mundane until you see what accumulates over centuries in a French country estate, this functions as both a renovation diary and an accidental archaeology of previous occupants' lives. It's the kind of slow, satisfying discovery video that works well if you like process over polish. A good entry point to the channel if you haven't followed their renovation journey.

items.title: I Tested Different Victorian Menstruation Products
items.note: Karolina Żebrowska goes beyond describing Victorian menstrual products and actually tests reconstructions of them, which shifts this from historical curiosity into something genuinely illuminating about daily life constraints women navigated. Her dry, unsentimental humor keeps it engaging rather than grim. It's the kind of history that makes you reconsider what 'getting dressed' used to involve.

items.title: The Weirdest Foods Ever Put in A Can
items.note: A light, quick tour through the genuinely strange history of what humanity has decided to preserve in tin, think whole chickens, cheeseburgers, and things you'd never expect to survive a shelf. It's the kind of video that works best as a casual lunch break watch. No deep insight, but reliably entertaining and mercifully short at 10 minutes.

items.title: Why everyone wants Greenland
items.note: In just over two minutes, Johnny Harris distills the geographic, strategic, and resource-based reasons why Greenland keeps appearing in geopolitical conversations, no filler, no padding. It's a rare case where the short format actually serves the topic, giving you exactly enough context to follow the news without a 20-minute explainer. Efficient and worth the two minutes.

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Clues, Symbolism, & the Ending of HIS & HERS EXPLAINED
items.note: If you finished the Netflix series and felt like you'd missed something, this breakdown maps the specific clues and symbolic choices the showrunners embedded around themes of marriage, prejudice, and class. Lucas Blue focuses on what the ending is actually arguing, not just recapping what happened. Worth watching alongside the show rather than instead of it.

items.title: Smart Gardeners Sow These Seeds in Late Winter
items.note: GrowVeg makes a concrete case for which seeds actually benefit from a late-winter indoor start versus which ones are a waste of your windowsill space. The advice is tied to real growing timelines, not just enthusiasm, so it's practical for anyone planning a vegetable garden. If you've ever started seeds too early and watched leggy seedlings collapse, this is worth your time.

items.title: We Adopted a PUPPY! (& other updates)
items.note: Rachel Maksy returns from a break to introduce a new puppy, tour a potential new workspace, and have an unscripted, warm conversation about where her creative work is heading. It's a low-stakes life-update video that rewards viewers who already enjoy her presence, though it offers little for newcomers looking for a project or tutorial. Think of it as checking in with a friend rather than learning something specific.

items.title: 30 Beluga Whales are Finally Being Rescued After 2 Years Waiting in Shuttered Theme Park
items.note: After two years stranded in a shuttered theme park, 30 beluga whales are finally getting the rescue they deserve, a reminder that sustained public pressure and dedicated conservationists can move mountains, even for the most overlooked animals. This is what it looks like when people simply refuse to give up.

items.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
items.note: Amid the noise of daily headlines, there are always quiet wins worth celebrating, and a weekly roundup dedicated to finding them is proof that good things keep happening whether or not we're paying attention.

items.title: 15 ‘Expert Flirts’ share their can’t-miss advice to help shy people flirt
items.note: Real human connection still matters, and the fact that people are sharing genuine, low-stakes ways to reach out to one another is a small but warm antidote to an increasingly isolated world.

items.title: ‘Food Really Is Medicine’
items.note: Growing evidence that food can function as medicine puts meaningful health tools back in everyday hands, shifting power toward prevention and away from purely reactive care.

items.title: A cheap, homegrown catnip lotion could replace DEET for millions of Ugandans
items.note: Researchers have found that catnip, an easy-to-grow backyard plant, produces a lotion that rivals DEET at repelling mosquitoes, offering a cheap, natural, and locally sourced protection against malaria for millions of Ugandans. It's a beautiful example of nature quietly holding solutions we're only beginning to notice.

items.title: Porcelain Room in Aranjuez, Spain
items.note: Visitors to Aranjuez often come for its royal gardens and the grandeur of the Royal Palace of Aranjuez. Yet one of the palace's most astonishing treasures is tucked away in a corner room that feels more like a theatrical illusion than a royal chamber. Completed in 1765, the Porcelain Room was the first major masterpiece created by the Royal Porcelain Factory established under Charles III of Spain.

items.title: Celestia in Laporte Township, Pennsylvania
items.note: Today, visitors to Celestia will find little more than a forest, a few traces of old foundations, and a historical marker along Route 42. In the mid-1800s, however, this mountaintop was intended to become something far more ambitious: a sacred city built in preparation for Christ's return. Founded by religious leader Peter E.

items.title: National Gallery of Armenia in Yerevan, Armenia
items.note: The National Gallery of Armenia is the largest fine arts museum in Armenia and home to the most comprehensive collection of Armenian art in the world. Founded in 1921, the Gallery preserves more than 42,000 works of Armenian and international fine art and decorative-applied arts. Its collections include masterpieces by world-renowned artists such as Hovhannes Aivazovsky, Vardges Sureniants, Arshile Gorky, Marc Chagall, Wassily Kandinsky, Donatello, Tintoretto, and many others.

items.title: Buzz Aldrin Used This Humble Felt-Tip Pen to Save the Apollo II Astronauts. It Just Sold for More Than $850,000
items.note: In July 1969, a quick-thinking Aldrin used the writing instrument to rearm the engine arm circuit of the lunar module "Eagle" after the switch broke off during the  moon landing

items.title: This Viking Longship Crossed the Atlantic Before Starring in Christopher Nolan's 'Odyssey' as Odysseus' Greek Galley
items.note: Hollywood stars including Matt Damon went to rowing camp to learn how to operate the vessel for the new film

items.title: This Meteorite Crashed Into a New Jersey Home in 2024. Now, Scientists Say It Contains Some of the Building Blocks of Life
items.note: The Hillsborough meteorite belongs to a rare class of rocks from space, according to a new study. It holds amino acids and other organic compounds, as well as evidence of salty water

items.title: Buckingham Palace's Royal Picture Gallery Revives the Salon-Style Hang, Doubling the Number of Masterpieces on View
items.note: Charles III reportedly had a say in the stylistic switch, which rearranged and added artworks by the likes of Rembrandt, Rubens, Vermeer and Van Dyck

items.title: Ancient Egyptian princesses knew their way around weapons of war
items.note: Analysis of six mummies from ancient Egypt challenges the idea that the bows and daggers buried with some royal women were merely ceremonial.

items.title: This “exotic weirdo” exoplanet has a rocky surface and an atmosphere
items.note: Astronomers found a helium atmosphere around a rocky exoplanet, a good sign for the search for life outside the solar system.

items.title: Thermodynamic Computers Go With the (Energy) Flow
items.note: Today’s computers need safeguards against random energy fluctuations. Thermodynamic computers would put those fluctuations to use. The post Thermodynamic Computers Go With the (Energy) Flow first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.title: Why Am I Left-Handed?
items.note: An invisible difference in 10% of humans poses deep mysteries in several fields at once. The post Why Am I Left-Handed? first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.title: We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow. Turns Out, Some Can Fracture.
items.note: Researchers thought that what enabled complex fluids to break apart was their elasticity. But a crack in a nonelastic simple fluid has them questioning that idea. The post We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow.

items.title: Will We Ever Find Alien Civilizations?
items.note: Astronomer David Kipping discusses why claims of extraterrestrial life keep dissolving under scrutiny, why we need a more statistically grounded approach to searching for life beyond Earth, and why it’s rational to believe that we may be alone. The post Will We Ever Find Alien Civilizations? first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.note: If you were to rip open a tattered matchbox, what might you find hidden in its confines? Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

items.note: Humans' futile control of time forms the basis of a new suite of works in Padeu's solo show at Larkin Durey. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

items.title: See Peter Marlow’s ‘The English Cathedral’ at London’s Oldest Gothic Church
items.note: Between 2010 and 2011, Marlow documented all 42 Anglican cathedrals across the breadth and length of England. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

items.title: Experience Ana Mendieta’s Visceral, ‘Neolithic’ Work in a Major Survey
items.note: A large-scale, immersive exhibition of her work opens today at Tate Modern. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

items.title: Diseases of the Will: Neuroscience Founding Father Santiago Ramón y Cajal on the Six Psychological Flaws That Keep the Gifted from Living Up to Their Gift
items.note: Santiago Ramón y Cajal, the man who mapped the architecture of the human brain, turns that same precision on the quiet self-sabotages that hollow out a talented life from the inside. If you've ever suspected your greatest obstacle is not circumstance but something more interior and harder to name, this is the essay to read with a cup of something warm and no interruptions.

items.title: Tiny Desk Radio: Air, Igor Levit, Fito Páez
items.note: The feeling of watching strangers celebrate in a sun-drenched plaza, flags waving, strangers briefly becoming family.

items.title: Tune in to a mini-concert with Weird Nightmare
items.note: Like discovering your favorite bruiser suddenly knows how to slow dance, and they're surprisingly good at it.

items.title: Dave Kendall, Creator of MTV’s 120 Minutes, Dies at 63
items.note: A late-night elegy for the flickering TV glow that once made misfits feel seen.

items.title: Michael Brook :: The Aquarium Drunkard Interview
items.note: The sonic equivalent of light refracting through deep water, cool and weightless and ancient.

items.title: Sombr’s “You Are the Reason Tour”: How to Get Last-Minute Tickets
items.note: The breathless scramble of last-minute plans that somehow always turn into the night you'll talk about for years.

items.title: Echo & the Bunnymen Announce New Album Apples for Isaac, Reveal Lead Single: Stream
items.note: Bittersweet and weather-beaten, like a letter written to someone who just barely missed reading it.

items.title: Fast Food Chains You Thought Were Gone
items.note: Rather than a standard eulogy for dead chains, this video flips the premise: these brands didn't actually die, they just became nearly invisible. It's a satisfying mix of nostalgia and genuine surprise for anyone who assumed certain childhood staples were long gone.

items.title: Historian Explores Famous Viking Sites in Iceland
items.note: Dan Snow visits the actual physical sites, longhouses, assembly plains, volcanic landscapes, rather than just narrating history from a studio, which gives the Viking settlement of Iceland a tangible sense of place. At 26 minutes it's long enough to feel like a proper trip without overstaying its welcome.

items.title: Fashion History Myths Are Kinda Sexist
items.note: Karolina Żebrowska zeroes in on a specific pattern: the historical myths that tend to survive are disproportionately the ones that frame women as passive victims of their own clothing. She backs the argument with specific examples and her dry, precise delivery keeps it from feeling like a lecture.

items.title: Why the US has so many undocumented immigrants
items.note: In under three minutes, Johnny Harris distills the structural reasons the US undocumented population stays large, not individual choices but system design, making it one of the more efficient explainers on a topic that usually generates more heat than clarity.

items.title: The harmony Carnie Wilson spent her whole life trying to find
items.note: Carnie Wilson recounts what it was like growing up inside Brian Wilson's world, music as both gift and chaos, and what she was actually chasing through all the public struggles she's been known for. It's a rare first-person account that reframes a familiar tabloid story as something quieter and more honest.

items.title: Hit By Storm Goretti
items.note: Storm Goretti hits the chateau mid-renovation, and this episode documents the real damage and the team's immediate response, not a dramatic retelling after the fact. For followers of the project it's a grounding reminder that restoring a 19th-century French chateau is genuinely unpredictable work.

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, & the Ending of 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
items.note: This is a structured breakdown of the symbolism and ending of *28 Years Later: The Bone Temple* for viewers who want to process what they just watched rather than just rewatch it. Lucas Blue focuses specifically on the religious and moral themes Nia DaCosta builds into the film, which aren't always obvious on a first viewing.

items.title: But what is cross-entropy? | Compression is Intelligence Part 2
items.note: Grant Sanderson builds a genuine understanding of cross-entropy, the loss function at the heart of LLM training, by grounding it in information theory and compression rather than just handing you a formula. If you've wanted to understand *why* models are trained the way they are, not just how, this 34 minutes will actually get you there.

items.title: Why Smart Gardeners Have Stopped Buying Potting Mix
items.note: The video makes a concrete, cost-driven case for making your own growing medium rather than buying bagged potting mix, walking through exactly what to combine and why. Practical enough to change what you do this season, especially as compost-based alternatives keep proving themselves in real garden trials.

items.title: Japanese Engineers Help Modernize India’s Massive Railway, Launching Her First Bullet Train
items.note: Two nations sharing engineering expertise across borders is a quiet but powerful reminder that collaboration can transform everyday life for millions of people. When a bullet train replaces a gruelling journey, it gives ordinary commuters something precious: time back with their families.

items.title: How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives
items.note: This programme shows that rebuilding a life after devastating injury is possible when people are given practical tools, land, and community. Growing food is not just about sustenance, it's about restoring dignity and a sense of future.

items.title: Hubbell Rubbell in Howard, Kansas
items.note: The small grass lot along Highway 99 is packed with Jerry Hubbell's welded folk-art creatures, a whole menagerie built from farm equipment, scrap metal, and whatever else he had lying around. The cowboy, the dinosaur, the tin man, the giant insect and the pig in glasses all sharing space in this rusty universe. Jerry Hubbell was a rancher who learned welding only to repair farm implements, then he turned that skill into decades of outsider art until health issues finally forced him to stop.

items.title: Man Sentenced for Stealing 'Rare and Unique' Chinese Texts, Valued at More Than $200,000, by Using Aliases, Fake IDs and Dummy Manuscripts
items.note: The works were stolen from the University of California, Los Angeles, over the course of several years

items.title: Giraffes Might Be Even Smarter Than We Thought. Some Can Solve Simple Math Problems, a New Study Suggests
items.note: Two of four tested giraffes seemed to be able to mentally combine numbers to get one of their favorite treats: carrots. But all the animals failed at tasks involving subtraction or multiple calculations

items.title: Astronomers Noticed a Mysterious Spot Appearing in Telescope Images. It Turned Out to Be the Faintest Exoplanet Ever Imaged From Earth
items.note: The gas giant, called Beta Pictoris d, had been hiding in plain sight for more than a decade, until two independent teams verified its existence

items.title: A.I. Music Is Already Here. To Protect Human Artists, the Record Industry Proposes New Labels, Like Those for Explicit Lyrics
items.note: The proposal comes as A.I.-generated music makes up an increasing share of what’s available on streaming platforms

items.title: An ancient Maya astronomer finally has a name
items.note: A Maya calendar formula bears the name Sak Tahn Waax, the first known Classic Maya mathematician-astronomer directly credited for such work.

items.title: Genes offer new clues to stopping Huntington’s disease in its tracks
items.note: A new generation of therapies aims to intervene at a recently discovered gap between the disease’s molecular march and its neurological consequences.

items.note: In an ingenious restoration project, the humble oyster shell is transformed into a powerful tool to halt coastal erosion - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

items.title: Loitering towards war
items.note: While Vienna’s coffeehouses bred modernism, in Belgrade’s kafanas grew conspiracy and rage. Their clash consumed Europe - by Anton Cebalo Read on Aeon

items.title: The Debate Over Pictures in Science
items.note: Botanical illustrations helped bring science to new audiences, but not every scientist welcomed the change. The post The Debate Over Pictures in Science appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: How to Bear Your Sorrows: Nick Cave on Integrating the Darkness of Loss with the Bright Ongoingness of Life
items.note: Nick Cave argues that grief isn't a wound to be healed but a capacity to be carried, and that the real surprise waiting on the other side of sorrow isn't recovery, but the world, still stubbornly, beautifully worlding. Set aside the half-hour and let it quietly dismantle the idea that you were ever supposed to get over anything.

items.title: Mallory Hawk – “Four O’Clocks”
items.note: The sound of watching military helicopters disappear into a grey childhood sky, years later, from a safe distance.

items.note: A fragile, candlelit cover that feels like a lifeline passed quietly between strangers.

items.note: Dusting off a mixtape you made at 19 and realizing it still hits exactly the same.

items.note: Weathered and warm like a late-night conversation on a porch with someone who's lived enough to say something true.

items.note: The thrill of finding a crumpled polaroid in a vintage shop that turns out to be of someone legendary.
