items.title: Humpback Whale Watching Surges in Brazil Where Population Has Grown 27-fold Since Whaling Ended
items.note: A 27-fold increase in humpback whales off Brazil's coast is a remarkable testament to what happens when we simply stop harming a species and let nature heal itself. The fact that communities can now build a thriving whale-watching culture where there was once industrial hunting is proof that recovery, even from near-extinction, is genuinely possible.

items.title: Gen Xer psychiatrist says we don’t actually miss the ’80s, we miss these 6 things
items.note: A psychiatrist's reminder that nostalgia is really a longing for specific feelings, connection, simplicity, autonomy, is quietly empowering, because those things aren't locked in the past. Understanding what we're actually craving gives us a real chance to cultivate it in our lives right now.

items.title: Pont de la Bâtiaz in Martigny, Switzerland
items.note: In the small city of Martigny is a wonderfully pleasant bridge named 'le Pont de la Bâtiaz'. Its outward beauty aside, it is the last wooden bridge in the entire French speaking canton of Valais that can still withstand vehicular usage. Spanning the Drance river, several different iterations of the bridge have existed at the location.

items.title: Rex, the Beach Blvd Dinosaur in Jacksonville, Florida
items.note: Rex the Dinosaur is regarded as one of Jacksonville’s most lovable roadside landmark. Built in the 1960s, he was originally installed as part of Goony Golf, a mini-golf and family-fun complex which closed its doors and putting greens in the late 1990s. When the land was purchased for redevelopment, locals protested his destruction and, in 2007, Rex was not only saved but restored with help from students in the University of North Florida's Building Construction Management program.

items.title: Blackjack Cemetery    in Taft, Oklahoma
items.note: Resting place of Sarah Rector, who was known as the richest Black girl in the world at one point. Resting place for Muscogee Creek Freedmen, and other Black children made wealthy by oil. In the middle of the historic Black town of Taft.

items.title: ’Tropfsteinmaschine, 1996-2496‘ in Hamburg, Germany
items.note: The Hamburger Kunsthalle houses almost 91,000 works of art. The most famous of these is undoubtedly Caspar David Friedrich’s 'Wanderer über dem Nebelmeer' (Wanderer Above the Sea of Fog). However, among the various collections on display, one modest piece might easily pass unnoticed in the Contemporary Art section.

items.note: George Saunders quietly dismantles the story we tell ourselves about love being a finite, once-given thing, and what it might mean to stop mistaking our patterns for our fate. Read it slowly, somewhere you won't be interrupted.

items.title: The Disastrous Downfall of KFC
items.note:
For decades, Kentucky Fried Chicken dominated the fast food scene with the top secret fried chicken recipe created by its larger-than-life founder, Colonel Harlan Sanders. But more recently, KFC has struggled to keep people interested in what it's selling. 

In this episode of Weird History Food, we dig through the corporate battles, declining quality, and broken branding to figure out exactly how one of America’s most beloved restaurant chains lost its way.

Have you turned your back on KFC? Le

items.title: BREAKING DOWN Every Cameo & Hidden Reference in The Moment (The Charli xcx Movie)
items.note: In this A24 The Moment Explained / Charli xcx The Moment Movie Easter Eggs & Analysis, I deep-dive analyze The Moment 2026, starring Charli xcx, Alexander Skarsgard, and Hailey Benton Gates, coming off of the hype of the brat album, brat summer, brat era, (including the Charli xcx Taylor Swift beef/feud/controversy), as we look forward to the release of the Wuthering Heights album and Wuthering Heights movie. Directed by Aidan Zamiri, the film is a perfect blend of mockumentary, drama, and comed

items.title: 3,200-yo Tomb Discovered in Luxor from the Era of Ramses
items.note: A 3,200-year-old tomb from the age of Ramses reminds us that human stories are still being uncovered, proof that the past has not finished teaching us. Every such discovery deepens our shared sense of where we come from.

items.title: The marginalised groups finding community through football
items.note: Football is doing what it has always done best, turning strangers into teammates and giving marginalised people a place where they genuinely belong. That kind of community, built through a shared game, is simple, powerful, and quietly transformative.

items.title: Mathellaneous in Emporia, Kansas
items.note: Tucked inside a Kansas university campus is a math museum where you're not just allowed but encouraged to touch everything, a rare invitation in a world of 'do not handle' placards. It may be the only place of its kind within a thousand miles, which somehow makes the whole Midwest feel a little more wonderfully strange.

items.title: In a First, Astronomers Find Sugar in the Space Between Stars, Providing New Clues About the Origins of Life on Earth
items.note: The building blocks of life's genetic code have now been found drifting in an interstellar dust cloud near the galaxy's heart, meaning the ingredients for DNA may be less an Earth invention than a cosmic commonplace. The universe, it seems, has been quietly stocking the pantry for a very long time.

items.title: Meet Sak Tahn Waax, a Maya Math Whiz Who Lived More Than 1,000 Years Ago and Left Behind a ‘Really Elegant, Complex’ Formula
items.note: For over a millennium, a Maya mathematician's elegant work endured in stone while their name stayed buried, until now, when archaeologists finally pulled Sak Tahn Waax out of anonymity. It's a rare thing: a face, and a mind, restored to a discovery that had always been theirs.

items.title: AI is not ready to fly solo in space
items.note: Science fiction has long handed the cockpit to AI, but researchers are finding that the gap between 'can process data' and 'can handle the genuinely unexpected' is vast, and in the coldness of space, that gap has consequences. For now, the humans aren't optional.

items.title: The diffusion pilot
items.note: An animator stares down a technology that can conjure in seconds what once took weeks, and asks the quietly devastating question: what is craft for, when effort is no longer the gatekeeper? His answer, hard-won and deeply human, is worth sitting with.

items.title: Life Inside Icelandic Turf Houses
items.note: Those romantically mossy Icelandic turf roofs were also perpetually damp, dimly lit, and shared with livestock, the pastoral dream and the actual lived reality being, as usual, entirely different things. Enchanting from the outside is a very old architectural tradition.

items.title: Fernando Pessoa on Unselfing into Who You Really Are
items.note: Fernando Pessoa spent his life fracturing himself into dozens of invented personas, and somehow arrived at a stranger, more honest self than most of us manage with all our sincerity intact. This is an essay about the paradox that losing yourself on purpose might be the only reliable way home.

items.title: Greg Mendez on his latest album, 'Beauty Land'
items.note: Like reading old journal entries in a quiet apartment while the city hums indifferently outside.

items.title: Josaleigh Pollett – “Bed Of Quiet”
items.note: The kind of song you play on repeat while staring at the ceiling, feeling everything and nothing at once.

items.title: Queens Of The Stone Age – “Easy Street” (Feat. Nikki Lane)
items.note: Desert moonlight and cigarette smoke drifting through an open window at 2 a.m.

items.title: Penelope Isles – “I Loved You, Robert Pattinson”
items.note: Dreamy, tongue-in-cheek devotion that floats like a crush you'd be embarrassed to admit but can't shake.

items.title: Friendship and Charlotte Cornfield Are Going on Tour Together
items.note: The anticipatory buzz of circling a tour date on a calendar and counting down the weeks.

items.title: Listen to Queens of the Stone Age’s First Single in Three Years
items.note: The chaotic warmth of a house party that somehow becomes a memory you cherish forever.

items.title: Lucinda Williams Announces North American Fall 2026 Headlining Tour
items.note: Worn-in boots on a long highway, with heartbreak weathered into something almost like wisdom.

items.title: How Much Are the Cheapest Tickets to See AC/DC on Their 2026 Tour?
items.note: The primal thrill of checking your bank account against a stadium ticket price and deciding it's absolutely worth it.

items.title: Forest Ecologist Answers Tree Questions | Tech Support | WIRED
items.note:
Forest conservation scientist Dominick DellaSala joins WIRED to answer the internet’s burning questions about trees. What did ancient forests look like? What do tree rings really prove? Do rainforests create rain or does rain create rainforests? Answers to these questions and many more await on Forest Support.

Join historians Dan Snow and Tim Maltin as they delve into the Titanic’s wreck site, guided by Magellan’s cutting-edge 2022 scan.

Built from over 700,000 individual photographs, this extraordinary “point cloud” is the most detailed scan of the Titanic ever captured, offering everyone the opportunity to explore the wreck from the safety of their own homes.

items.title: Why Canadians Are Turning Their Backs On Tim Hortons
items.note:
To Canadians, Tim Hortons is more than a coffee shop; it's a symbol of Canada itself. From hockey culture to political campaigns, “Timmies” was seemingly everywhere. But behind the double-doubles and Timbits, the company has found itself facing controversy, consumer complaints about declining quality, angry franchise owners, and a major identity crisis.

In this episode of Weird History Food, we head north to take a good hard look at everything that's gone wrong for Tim Hortons.

Are you a curre

items.title: Every Detail and Cast Member Response to the Backlash of Wuthering Heights
items.note:
In this Emerald Fennell Wuthering Heights Controversy Backlash Explained Video Essay & Analysis, I deep-dive analyze the adaptation issues with Wuthering Heights 2026, starring Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi. Directed by Emerald Fennell, the film infamously race-swapped (whitewashed) its lead Heathcliff character and heavily sexualized the classic Wuthering Heights novel.
In this video, I want to investigate Emerald Fennel’s response to the controversy at every moment up until the release of the

items.title: Analysing the Tradwife trend... through the lens of JoJo Siwa
items.note:
JoJo Siwa’s journey from rainbow queer icon to vintage feminine aesthetics has sparked conversation online, and it intersects with the rise of “tradwife” content across TikTok and Instagram. In this video essay, I explore the so-called “JoJo Siwa to Tradwife Pipeline,” what the tradwife trend represents culturally, and what it might mean for queer and disabled communities.

Why does tradwife content feel comforting to some queer people, but deeply unsettling to others?
What does nostalgia mean

Dr Di Wang is an art historian and curator. Her work focuses on art and culture of East Asia from the late-nineteenth century to the present.

Following her doctorate at the University of Oxford, she has taught as a departmental lecturer and research associate at the Department of History of Art at Oxford. Her first book project reveals the surprising entanglement of medicine, science, and revolutionar

items.title: Coffee Associated with Lower Risks of Liver Cancer and Deadly Cirrhosis
items.note: New research suggests that one of the world's most beloved daily rituals may offer genuine protection against serious liver disease, a quiet reminder that good news sometimes hides in plain sight, right there in your morning cup. Science continues to find unexpected allies in the fight for long-term health.

items.title: Woman with cancer apologizes to Rihanna for her appearance. Rihanna has the best response.
items.note: Rihanna's response is a small but powerful act of human kindness that reminds us how a single moment of genuine compassion, especially from someone in the spotlight, can restore a person's dignity and lift them when they need it most.

items.title: Al-Saada in Al Ghayl, Saudi Arabia
items.note: Before Romeo and Juliet, there was Layla and Majnun, a real, documented love story from 7th-century Arabia so devastating it inspired poets from Persia to the Ottoman Empire for centuries. The village where it unfolded still exists, quietly, 300 kilometers from Riyadh.

items.title: Lafcadio Hearn Japanese Gardens in Tramore, Ireland
items.note: Lafcadio Hearn spent boyhood summers in an Irish seaside town before becoming one of the Western world's most lyrical interpreters of Japanese ghost stories, and now a hidden garden in that same town quietly keeps his memory alive behind a stone wall most locals walk past without a second glance.

items.title: The Grave of Dušan Vasiljev in Kikinda, Serbia
items.note: Dušan Vasiljev poured the trauma of the First World War into verse that reshaped Serbian literature, yet his grave in one of Kikinda's oldest cemeteries is a modest white stone, easy to miss among the grander tombs of people history has already forgotten.

items.title: An English King's Bones Were Moved Over Centuries and Eventually Lost. One Researcher Claims He Discovered Their Location—Under a Parking Lot
items.note: Alfred the Great, the king who literally saved England from Viking conquest, may have spent centuries beneath a parking lot, which is either darkly comic or deeply fitting for a civilization that keeps misplacing its greatest dead.

items.title: Here's How These Adorable Mice Can Live at an Extremely High Elevation Where No Other Mammal Is Known to Reside
items.note: The Andean leaf-eared mouse has quietly claimed the highest known mammal habitat on Earth, a volcanic summit at 22,000 feet where the air is so thin and cold that survival itself seems like a biological argument it shouldn't be winning.

items.title: This 700-Year-Old English Church Nearly Closed—but Then the Vicar Discovered a Stash of Gold Coins Under the Altar
items.note: A 700-year-old English church on the verge of closing its doors was saved not by a diocese or a donor drive, but by a box of gold bullion discovered under the altar just before Easter, which is either a miracle or extraordinary timing, depending on your persuasion.

items.title: How Has Roman Concrete Lasted for Millennia? A 1,900-Year-Old Latrine Offers New Clues About the Material's Impressive Durability
items.note: Roman concrete has been quietly healing its own cracks for nearly two millennia through a chemical process called carbonation, meaning ancient builders accidentally engineered a self-repairing material that modern construction is only now beginning to understand.

items.title: A new sort of Alzheimer’s drug shows glimmers of promise
items.note: An experimental Alzheimer's drug that targets tau, the protein that tangles and chokes neurons, showed genuine signs of slowing memory loss in clinical trials, with a surprise twist in the data that researchers are still working to explain.

items.title: How to Grow Old: Bertrand Russell on What Makes a Fulfilling Life
items.note: Russell's quiet prescription for aging well isn't about acceptance or resignation, it's about the slow, deliberate dissolving of the self into something larger than personal fortune or fear. If you've ever wondered what it might feel like to stop clutching at life and simply open your hands, this is the essay to read with a cup of something warm.

items.title: Baby Rose returns with new album, 'Yearnalism'
items.note: Late-night candle-burning music for tracing the outline of everything you've ever wanted but couldn't quite name.

items.title: Courtney Marie Andrews – “Carolina Caroline” (Jonathan Edwards Cover)
items.note: Like finding a handwritten letter tucked inside a secondhand book, warm, unhurried, and quietly meant for you.

items.title: Julia Jacklin – “I Wish”
items.note: The kind of song that hits you in the chest on a Tuesday when you thought you were fine.

items.title: Jake Xerxes Fussell Announces New Album The Old Beloved Path: Hear “Rock Island Line”
items.note: Dust rising off a gravel road at dusk, where the past and present share a seat on the same freight train.

items.title: ALBUM REVIEW: Neon Moons Make Their Honky-Tonk Debut With 'Day Late & a Dollar Short'
items.note: Neon beer signs and scuffed dance floors where everyone knows the words and nobody's pretending.

items.title: FRESH TRACK: Andrew Sa - "Lavender Cowboy"
items.note: A twilight drive through open country with a voice so tender it makes the ache feel almost beautiful.

items.title: THROUGH THE LENS: From Australia to Pennsylvania, Five Festivals Offered a Variety of Ways to Explore Roots Music
items.note: The feeling of stumbling into a field full of strangers who all somehow became your people by sundown.

items.title: Jake Xerxes Fussell Lines Up New Album The Old Beloved Path
items.note: Weathered wood and railroad smoke, old American mythology reimagined with mud still on its boots.

items.title: Jess Williamson Readies New Album A Mile South of Heaven
items.note: Standing at the edge of something ending with your face tilted up like you're daring it to rain.

items.title: Alden Hellmuth :: Tether
items.note: The sonic equivalent of a shove in a crowded subway car that somehow turns into the best conversation you've had all year.

Thanks to a viral video showing off his unusually stylish meat slicing technique and now legendary salt sprinkle, the Turkish butcher and restauranteur Nusret Gökçe shot to fame as "Salt Bae." But thanks to a series of mistakes, missteps, and plain old bad decisions, the internet's love affair with Gökçe proved short-lived. 

In this episode of Weird History Food, we're peeling back the hype to take a look at the bizarre rise and fall of the world’s most dramatic chef.

Did you ever eat at one o

items.title: So What's Going on With the "Wuthering Heights" (2026) Costumes? [Review]
items.note: I don’t have much material to work with screenshot-wise as the movie wasn’t released online yet, so you’ll have to watch it to see some of the costumes/styles I'm discussing!!

items.title: What positive change have you seen unfold over many years?
items.note: Long-term witnesses to change remind us that progress is real but slow, and that paying attention over years, not just news cycles, reveals a world quietly improving in ways we too easily miss.

items.title: Musée des Années 30  in Boulogne-Billancourt, France
items.note: This wonderful museum in Boulogne-Billancourt (west of Paris, but accessible by Metro) is devoted to the art and atmosphere of the 1930s, bringing together furniture, paintings, sculpture, ceramics, posters, and decorative objects from the interwar period. What makes it especially compelling is that it does not treat Art Deco as a purely glamorous style. The museum also reflects the world that produced it: a France fascinated by speed, industry, cinema, architecture, empire, and modern life.

items.title: The Birthplace of Kool-Aid
items.note: Listen and subscribe on Apple Podcasts , Spotify , and all major podcast apps. Walk with me for a bit through the halls of the Hastings Museum in South Central Nebraska. You can see models of the dinosaurs that once roamed the Great Plains.

items.title: Ancient Romans Put Their Faith in Guardian Spirits. Archaeologists Just Found a Rare 1,600-Year-Old Carving of One in Northern England
items.note: Known as a “genius,” the protective spirit or deity was believed to bring good fortune and prosperity to a particular person, household or place. Archaeologists haven't found many depictions of them across Roman Britain

items.title: Meet the Earliest Known 'Right-Handed' Animal, a Worm-Like Creature That Lived About 550 Million Years Ago
items.note: During the Ediacaran period, the critter wriggled around on the ocean floor of what’s now South Australia and preferred to turn right, a fossil analysis suggests

items.title: Thomas Jefferson’s Original Rough Draft of the Declaration of Independence Can Now Be Seen at the Library of Congress
items.note: The new exhibition, which shows how the document was edited, also features an early copy of the Gettysburg Address

items.title: A new map traces the sky’s water highways
items.note: Atmospheric rivers bring heavy rain and floods, but if they don’t come around, it could mean drought. A new global map reveals little-known pathways.

items.title: A quasar breaks the record for most distant supermassive black hole
items.note: The Euclid space telescope discovery could help researchers understand how black holes grew so massive so quickly in the early universe.

items.title: Archie G. Norcross’ Maine Forest Fire Maps (1918–22)
items.note: Circular maps custom-made for the fire towers that once dotted the American wilderness.

items.title: The Prophecies of the Brahan Seer (1877)
items.note: Historical predictions from the Scottish Nostradamus.

items.title: “Plaything of the Gods”: Photographs of Pushball (early 1900s)
items.note: Photographs and films of a forgotten pastime.

items.title: The looting of science fiction
items.note: Tech titans claim the genre inspired them. But all they’ve done is graft their politics onto stories of a better future - by Ali Rıza Taşkale Read on Aeon

items.title: What Was Sewer Socialism?
items.note: Milwaukee mayor Daniel Hoan believed the best argument for socialism was a city government that worked. The post What Was Sewer Socialism? appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: How to Help Someone Change: The Samurai Guide to Giving Feedback
items.note: This essay wrestles with one of love's quietest frustrations: watching someone you believe in repeatedly get in their own way, and what a centuries-old samurai tradition might teach us about the near-impossible art of helping them see it. It's less about feedback techniques than about the courage and tenderness it takes to tell someone a hard truth without breaking what's between you.

items.title: ALBUM REVIEW: Aaron Lee Tasjan Celebrates the Outsiders and Also-Rans on 'Get Over It, Underdog'
items.note: The sound of someone who's been counted out their whole life finally dancing alone in their kitchen and not caring who sees.

items.title: Yesternow: Editor’s Note Volume Eight
items.note: Late-night LA heat with the fan on low, records sweating on the turntable, nowhere to be until morning.

items.title: The Stray Cats Extend 2026 US Tour with New Fall Dates
items.note: Greased-back rebellion for people who still believe a great rockabilly riff can fix almost anything.

items.title: Stream Terence Blanchard’s “Magnetic”
items.note: The kind of sophisticated warmth that fills a dimly lit room where everyone in it knows exactly what they're listening to.

items.title: GChatting with Vijay Iyer about ‘Holding It Down’
items.note: Cerebral and quietly urgent, like a conversation you keep returning to even after the night has wound down.

items.title: Was Agincourt The Most BRUTAL Medieval Battle in History?
items.note: This isn't just a retelling of Agincourt's famous upset, History Hit digs into why heavily armored French knights were so catastrophically vulnerable to English longbowmen on that particular terrain. At 51 minutes it's a proper deep-dive, worth it if you've ever wondered what "heavily outnumbered" actually meant in medieval battlefield terms and how mud changed the calculus entirely.

items.title: I time travelled to Ancient Rome! (Vlog)
items.note: Chloe structures a genuine day-in-the-life of 44 BC Rome, communal toilets, garum, strigils, a live gladiator vote, making ancient social norms feel viscerally real rather than textbook-distant. The Julius Caesar framing is a hook, but the sensory specificity is what actually sticks.

items.title: a bullsh*t motivational podcast in 1895
items.note: In two and a half minutes, Karolina Żebrowska recreates what a hustle-culture podcast episode would have sounded like in 1895, and it's uncomfortably identical to what's in your feed today. The joke lands because the research is real; the emptiness of the genre hasn't changed at all.

items.title: Why Emily Brontë Cared About Race | "Wuthering Heights" (2026) Review
items.note: Jessica Kellgren-Fozard uses the upcoming Emerald Fennell adaptation as a lens to examine something scholars have argued about for decades: whether Heathcliff's ambiguous Liverpool origins were Emily Brontë's deliberate commentary on race and empire. The video is specific about Victorian racial hierarchies rather than just gesturing at them, which makes it genuinely useful context whether or not you plan to see the film.

items.title: Talking news and media with Max Fisher
items.note: Johnny Harris and Max Fisher, two journalists who've spent years inside legacy media and independent YouTube, compare notes on how news incentives warp coverage in ways audiences rarely see from the outside. It's a candid, shop-talk conversation rather than a polished explainer, which makes it more useful.

items.title: The most beautiful formula not enough people understand
items.note: Grant Sanderson builds toward a formula connecting the volumes of spheres across every dimension, a result that's genuinely surprising, using animation that makes the leap from 3D intuition to n-dimensional abstraction feel earned rather than hand-waved. At an hour it asks for real attention, but the payoff is one of those rare moments where abstract math produces a result that's aesthetically beautiful.

items.title: The Smart Way to Grow Strawberries (No Slugs!)
items.note: GrowVeg walks through exactly which raised bed configurations and physical barriers actually keep slugs off strawberries, with specific product recommendations and spacing logic rather than vague organic-gardening platitudes. If you've lost a crop to slugs before, the timing and placement advice here is genuinely actionable.

items.title: From Ruin to DREAM HOME Chateau Master Suite - Start to Finish (Satisfying Time-lapses)
items.note: This start-to-finish renovation of a chateau master suite is structured around satisfying time-lapses that compress months of plaster, stone, and carpentry into a coherent visual arc, you can actually track the decision-making, not just the glamour shots. If you watch renovation content for the process logic rather than the lifestyle fantasy, the before-and-after progression here is unusually well-documented.

items.title: Which Orange Soda Is The Definitive Orange Soda?
items.note: A blind taste test ranking of Fanta, Sunkist, Crush, and the rest, with the specific question of why every orange soda lands on that exact same traffic-cone hue. It's a light 15 minutes that's more curious than it sounds, the flavor chemistry tangent alone justifies the runtime.

items.title: 15-yo Creates App for Reporting Potholes to the Government, and Uses AI to Help with Follow-up
items.note: A 15-year-old didn't just complain about a problem, he built a practical tool to fix it, proving that young people are already creating real solutions for the communities around them. It's a quiet reminder that civic ingenuity isn't waiting for the next generation; it's already here.

items.title: Happiness expert explains why ‘imposter syndrome’ is a good thing and how to lean into it
items.note: Reframing imposter syndrome as a signal of growth rather than a flaw is the kind of grounded reassurance most of us genuinely need, it means that feeling out of your depth is often a sign you're exactly where you should be.

items.title: Hoffmann Lime Kilns in Langcliffe, England
items.note: This is a fascinating Victorian feature of the Yorkshire Dales National Park, which details the history of lime production in the area and includes various ruined structures, such as the Hoffmann Kiln from 1873. This is regarded as the best preserved example of this building in England and was operational until 1931. The site was constructed next to the Settle-Carlisle railway for ease of transportation and now houses a modern business park and archaeology trail at the base of Stainforth Scar.

items.title: This T. Rex Fossil Could Fetch the Largest Sum of Any Dinosaur Ever Auctioned. Scientists Worry They'll Lose the Chance to Study It
items.note: Bids on "Gus" will start at $19 million, a steep price for public institutions. Specimens in private collections can be harder for researchers to examine, and they're practically impossible to include in studies in top-tier scientific journals

items.title: Artemis 1's Orion Spacecraft Withstood the Heat of Re-entry in a Critical Test for Humans' Return to the Moon. Now, NASA Will Loan the Historic Capsule to the Smithsonian's National Air and Space Museum
items.note: The exact timeline for the spacecraft's display hasn’t been announced, but the artifact will fit into an upcoming exhibition on how humans have built a long-term presence in Earth orbit and laid the groundwork for farther space travel

items.title: The Largest Operating Steam Locomotive in the World Weighs 1.2 Million Pounds. Now, the Historic Train May Be Coming to Your Town
items.note: Built in the 1940s, Big Boy No. 4014 retired in 1961 after traveling for more than one million miles. It's now making its way across the country in honor of America's 250th birthday

items.title: The Launch of Our Mid-Year Fundraiser!
items.note: Our Mid-Year Fundraiser is launched, and the new postcards theme will be Soil.

items.title: Covers from Cerîde-i Adliye, a Turkish Law Journal (1924–26)
items.note: Legal infographics that reflect a Turkish nation in flux.

items.title: Animal, Vegetable, Lamb: The Zoophyte from Tartary
items.note: In the Middle Ages, rumours spread across Europe of a strange hybrid creature, half-animal, half-plant, known as the Vegetable Lamb of Tartary. A hoax based on fern rhizomes? A euphemism for culled fetal lambs?

items.title: Harold A. Taylor’s Autochromes of California Flowers (early 20th century)
items.note: Photographs of flowers by an advocate of California’s beauty.

items.title: Karl Hess: toward liberty
items.note: ‘Society, in fact, is neighbourhoods’, how Karl Hess transformed from a Republican speechwriter into a radical welder - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

items.title: The anguish of choice
items.note: In the shattered aftermath of war, Sartre delivered a formidable lecture on freedom and meaning. Its urgency remains - by Skye C Cleary Read on Aeon

items.title: Rediscovering The Jewish Gazette
items.note: A rare archive reveals how Ireland’s Jewish community navigated identity, culture, and rising antisemitism in the 1930s. The post Rediscovering The Jewish Gazette appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: How Indonesia’s Feminists Use the Internet
items.note: Online platforms help Muslim scholars and activists share feminist interpretations of Islam, expanding access to new audiences. The post How Indonesia’s Feminists Use the Internet appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: This Prehistoric Giant Salamander Grew to Almost 4 Feet Long
items.note: A four-foot salamander once shared the planet with us, and its living relative is still out there, this piece traces the quiet, stubborn persistence of a creature that refused to become a fossil. It's a story about what survival actually looks like across deep time.

items.title: Flea and the Honora Band: Tiny Desk Concert
items.note: The sound of a virtuoso finally exhaling, loose, warm, and gloriously unshackled from the arena.

items.title: The Shins Celebrate 30th Anniversary With Previously Unreleased Songs On New 7″
items.note: Like finding an old mixtape in a jacket pocket you forgot you owned, full of songs that still know exactly how you felt.

items.title: Amy Helm No Longer Part Of Levon Helm Studios Amid “Disturbing Circumstances”
items.note: The heavy, unresolved ache of a family home with the lights slowly going out.

items.title: Ty Segall – “Running To Nowhere”
items.note: Distortion-soaked and restless, like sprinting through a city at midnight for no particular reason.

items.title: You Make The Choice :: On 30 years of Dusk at Cubist Castle
items.note: A bittersweet séance for a weird, beautiful subculture that existed before the algorithm ate everything.

items.title: Geddy Lee’s Voice Sounds Strong as RUSH Return to Stage Following His Laryngitis Battle: Watch
items.note: The triumphant roar of a crowd that held its breath for weeks finally getting to let it out.

items.title: Gerald Clayton Discusses his ‘Two-Shade’ Album
items.note: Late-afternoon jazz club energy, measured, cerebral, with something quietly burning underneath.

items.title: Jose James and Kris Bowers Live at Sculler’s in Boston
items.note: Velvet-voiced and alive, the kind of live jazz that makes a small room feel infinite.

items.title: Libstems Releases ‘Daydream Sounds’
items.note: Sunlit and unhurried, like drifting through a city you don't know with nowhere you need to be.

items.title: Pianist and Curator Aaron Diehl Does Miles Davis Centennial Right with Jazz in July, at 92NY
items.note: White-glove elegance meeting deep historical reverence, the kind of concert that makes you sit up straighter without being asked.

items.title: Murder at the Regatta
items.note: The History Guy digs up a 19th-century murder case so sensational it actually reshaped American legal thinking around intent and mental state in criminal trials. The story involves a love triangle at a rowing regatta, a killing that gripped the press, and a defense strategy that was genuinely novel for its time. If you like true crime with real historical stakes, this is a compact 20 minutes well spent.
items.source: The History Guy: History Deserves to Be Remembered

items.title: I time travelled to the Titanic 1912! (Vlog)
items.note: Chloe visits the Titanic museum experience in Belfast and uses it as an entry point to talk about the actual passengers, not the ship's engineering or the iceberg logistics, in a way that genuinely lands emotionally without being maudlin. The vlog format keeps it grounded, and her narration of specific individual stories gives the numbers a human weight that most documentaries miss.

items.title: S3 Ep1: How To Be a Victorian Servant
items.note: Karolina reads directly from an 1845 domestic service manual, which turns out to be a far more revealing primary source than any summary could be, the language itself shows exactly what was expected of young women entering service. What's striking is her argument that for some women, the structured role genuinely offered stability and even status, which complicates the easy "it was awful" narrative.

items.title: Medieval Sex According To Eleanor Janega
items.note: Dr. Eleanor Janega is one of the sharpest medieval historians working in public-facing media, and this conversation specifically dismantles the Victorian-era rewriting of medieval sexuality as uniformly repressed and shameful. The discussion gets into church doctrine versus actual recorded behavior, and Janega's specificity, she cites real cases and texts, keeps it from feeling like a broad generalization exercise.

items.title: The New "Ancient Aliens: Origins" Is Insane - Pt. 2
items.note: Miniminuteman doesn't just mock Ancient Aliens, he pauses specific claims and explains what the actual archaeology says, making this a genuine education in pseudoscience tactics disguised as entertainment. At over an hour, it's a commitment, but the running commentary format makes it more watchable than a straight debunking lecture, and you'll come away with a clearer sense of how these shows manufacture credibility.

items.title: How a nuke destroys a city
items.note: In under three minutes, Johnny Harris uses clear animation to walk through the concentric zones of destruction a nuclear detonation produces, blast radius, heat, radiation, scaled to a real city map. It's not alarmist; it's just a precise, visual answer to a question most people have but never look up.

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, and Artistry in A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms
items.note: If you've watched A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms and wanted someone to map the deliberate visual and thematic callbacks to the broader GRRM universe, this is a thorough attempt at exactly that. Lucas Blue goes symbol by symbol rather than just summarizing plot, which makes it more useful as a companion piece than as a standalone watch.

items.title: The Strange Cult Of Whataburger
items.note: Rather than just cataloging Texan loyalty as a quirk, this video actually investigates what operational and cultural decisions made Whataburger stick when dozens of regional chains didn't. It covers specific scandals the chain survived and how its ownership structure shaped its identity, useful context whether you've eaten there or just want to understand regional brand devotion.

items.title: This Little-Known Flower Banishes Pests from Your Garden
items.note: In under seven minutes, GrowVeg introduces limnanthes (poached egg plant) as a specific, evidence-backed companion flower that attracts predatory insects to handle aphids naturally. The video is practical and densely informative, no filler, and gives you a concrete plant to add to your garden plan this season.

items.title: Firm Breaks Ground on First Next-Gen Nuclear Reactor in America to Power Data Centers for Less
items.note: A new generation of smaller, safer nuclear reactors is moving from blueprint to reality in America, offering a cleaner way to meet our growing energy needs. This milestone suggests that genuinely low-carbon power, reliable enough to run entire data centers, is closer than many dared hope.

items.title: Lai Tak Tsuen Estate in Hong Kong
items.note: Built in 1975, Lai Tak Tsuen was one of Hong Kong's first public housing developments. Spread across the estate's tower blocks are 2600 individual dwellings, housing approximately 11,000 residents. Located on a hillside, the estate's most distinguishing features are the two bicylindrical towers, uniquely shaped amongst Hong Kong's public housing estates.

items.title: Friedrich Ebert Memorial in Heidelberg, Germany
items.note: Tucked away in the heart of Heidelberg stands the birthplace of Friedrich Ebert, Germany's first democratically elected president. What looks like a modest 19th-century home tells the story of a man who rose from a working-class background to help shape modern German democracy. The memorial site combines a historic house with a modern exhibition that explores the turbulent years of the early 20th century, the birth of the Weimar Republic, and the challenges of building a democratic state.

items.title: Cancer Cases Are Expected to Nearly Double by 2050, Warns the WHO. Low-Income Countries Will Be Disproportionately Affected
items.note: In a new report, the World Health Organization predicts that new annual diagnoses will rise from about 20.6 million today to almost 35 million by 2050. The biggest increases will be in Africa and the Eastern Mediterranean region

items.title: Nearly 150 Gray Whales Have Been Found Dead Along North America's Pacific Shore This Year, Prompting Scientists to Sound the Alarm
items.note: The population of eastern gray whales, which annually migrate along the West Coast between the Arctic and Mexico, has been declining in recent years. Conservationists say 2026 could be one of the deadliest years on record for the marine mammals

items.title: How Did Two Wolves End Up on This Remote Island Thousands of Years Ago? Researchers Think Humans Brought Them There, Then Cared for Them
items.note: The discovery suggests humans may have maintained relationships with wolves long after domesticated dogs came on the scene

items.title: Whether lefty or righty, practice makes the difference
items.note: Researchers looking for the origins of left-right dominance in the brain found no innately better motor skills on either side.

items.title: Meet the Milky Way’s puffiest planets
items.note: Two “superpuff” planets orbiting a sunlike star over 1,000 light-years from Earth are as big as Jupiter and as dense as cotton candy.

items.title: Diagrams for Travel from the Qanoon-e-Islam (1832)
items.note: Diagrams from a now-lost Urdu manuscript illustrating the propitious days and directions for travel.

items.title: The Art of Kite Flying (1430–1929)
items.note: 69 images of kite flying from across 5 centuries and some dozen countries.

items.title: Venetian Bridge Brawls in 17th and 18th Century Art
items.note: Artworks from an era when Venetians brawled on bridges.

items.title: Genevieve Stebbins’ Delsarte System of Dramatic Expression (1886)
items.note: A philosophical system and a practical guide for the cultivation of dramatic genius.

items.title: Patricia Evangelista on journalism
items.note: The ‘worst’ and ‘best possible’ job at once: what it’s like to dedicate your life to documenting crimes against humanity - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

items.title: Unmothered
items.note: We are each a living story, written with those who raised us. But when my most loving co-author ghosted me, my story fractured - by David Robson Read on Aeon

items.title: ‘The emperor is far away’
items.note: Ming Dynasty China left us copious texts, but these veil the lives of the vast majority of its people from our view - by Craig Clunas Read on Aeon

items.title: Skill nostalgia
items.note: Is all the beekeeping, baking and leatherwork just escapist fantasy or the start of a radically human approach to work? - by Joshua Habgood-Coote Read on Aeon

items.title: The Rise and Fall of the Public Sandbox
items.note: A nineteenth-century experiment in children's play helped reshape American cities, and later fell out of favor. The post The Rise and Fall of the Public Sandbox appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: Rudyard Kipling’s Buddhism
items.note: Long read as an imperial novel, Kim also reflects Britain's changing ideas about Buddhist thought and spiritual practice. The post Rudyard Kipling’s Buddhism appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: The Return of Florida’s Wild Flamingos
items.note: New sightings suggest a surprising ecological comeback, and a chance to reexamine the state's environmental past. The post The Return of Florida’s Wild Flamingos appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: Celebrating the Fourth of July
items.note: Take a moment to contemplate the history and complexity of Independence Day, American Style. The post Celebrating the Fourth of July appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

items.title: The House With No Story
items.note: Atlas Obscura CEO Louise Story didn't have a plan in Bloomington. She just had the app, her kids, and an open afternoon, and ended up somewhere she never expected. We picked Bloomington because of friends.

items.title: Stuck in the Long Valley Caldera
items.note: Driving through snowy mountains, Kelly’s hot spring adventure suddenly went wrong, or did it go right? Listen and subscribe on Apple Podcasts , Spotify , and all major podcast apps. A few weeks ago, I was up in the Sierra Nevada mountains by myself for the weekend.

items.title: Pythagoras on the Purpose of Life and the Meaning of Wisdom
items.note: The man who gave us the word 'philosopher' also had something quietly radical to say about why we're here at all, and it has nothing to do with achievement. A good one to read slowly, maybe twice.

items.title: A newly issued Oscar Peterson jazz album takes us back in time to a Detroit nightclub
items.note: Cigarette smoke and candlelight, the kind of night that only exists now on tape.

items.title: Jack White Joined by Daughter Scarlett White on Bass for Three Songs in Brooklyn
items.note: The rare, electric warmth of watching something become a family legacy in real time.

items.title: The Cure’s Simon Gallup Sidelined from Tour After Falling Ill, Son Eden Filling In
items.note: Bittersweet and a little haunting, like a torch passed through darkened stage wings.

items.title: AC/DC Kick Off Summer 2026 North American Tour with 21-Song Set of Classics: Video + Setlist
items.note: Pure throttle and nostalgia, the kind of stadium thunder that vibrates in your sternum long after the lights go down.

items.title: Linda May Han Oh – ‘Walk Against Wind’
items.note: The feeling of walking into cold wind with your eyes closed and somehow finding it peaceful.

items.title: Stream Saxophonist Chase Baird’s Lead Single “Ripcord” Off ‘A Life Between’ (Premiere)
items.note: The breathless momentum of a life accelerating toward something just out of frame.

items.title: Jason Moran Live With Theaster Gates (Concert Review)
items.note: Art and jazz colliding in a grand hall, leaving you staring at the ceiling long after you've gone home.

items.title: How A Ghostbusters Drink Became The Weirdest Drink Of The '90s
items.note:
Originally launched as a Ghostbusters-themed Hi-C drink in the 1980s, Ecto Cooler became one of the most beloved kids beverages of all time. It even survived for a while after the Ghostbusters franchise faded away. Nonetheless, Hi-C eventually discontinued it and that should’ve been the end... but it wasn't.

On this episode of Weird History Food, we're taking a look at how Ecto Cooler became a pop culture legend that fans kept bringing back from the dead.

Were you a fan of Ecto Cooler? Let us

items.title: The data black hole at the center of AI
items.note:
It is easy to forget how much data these models are trained on, and how much more it is than what we humans see in our lifetimes. We see these AIs as a galaxy glittering with capabilities, but at their center, invisible to the naked eye, holding all the constellations together, is an unimaginably massive black hole of data.
